title: Novel Non-destructive Evaluation of Corrosion on Rebar Embedded in Mortar Wall Using 10-GHz Doppler Sensor
authors: Taisei Watanabe, Ryosuke Suga
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radar Systems
abstract: This paper proposes a novel non-destructive method to detect corrosion on rebar embedded in a mortar wall using a 10-GHz Doppler sensor. The movement of the transmitting and receiving antennas parallel to the wall induced a Doppler shift, which suppressed unwanted reflections from the wall surface. This approach isolates the signal attenuation caused by rebar corrosion, enabling its direct measurement. The attenuation of 14 dB was observed due to the round-trip propagation in a 50 mm thick mortar wall, though the reflected level from the rebar can be detected. An attenuation of 7.8 dB was observed for a rebar with a 9 mm diameter and a corrosion level of around 100 mg/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> placed behind a 50 mm thick mortar wall.

title: Topology-Constrained Representation Dynamics for Robust and Stable Transformer Learning
authors: V Jothi Prakash, S Arul Antran Vijay, Gopikrishnan Sundaram
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Transformer architectures have achieved remarkable progress in natural language understanding, yet their internal representations often evolve in unregulated ways, leading to semantic drift and poor generalization under distributional shifts. This paper introduces the Topology-Constrained Representation Dynamics (TCRD) framework, a feedback-regulated transformer architecture that stabilizes representation evolution through geometric and cognitive control. Unlike conventional regularization applied at the parameter or embedding level, TCRD enforces smooth inter-layer manifold transitions via a differentiable Wasserstein-based continuity loss and ensures semantic coherence across domains through cognitive alignment regularization. Theoretically, TCRD establishes bounded representational drift and domain-invariant convergence, providing a formal connection between topological stability and neural learning dynamics. Empirical evaluation on three social reasoning benchmarks—HateXplain, GoEmotions, and PHEME—demonstrates that TCRD consistently outperforms strong transformer and topology-aware baselines, achieving average gains of +5.4% in Macro-F1 and +5.8% in AUC-ROC while reducing representational drift by 23.6%. Comprehensive ablation, cross-domain transfer, and statistical analyses confirm the framework’s scalability, interpretability, and stability. TCRD advances a control-theoretic perspective on transformer learning, showing how feedback-informed geometric regularization yields robust, cognitively coherent, and topologically stable neural representations suitable for real-world intelligent systems.

title: Mitigating Hallucination in Multimodal Information Systems: A Comparative Analysis of Modular LLM Architectures
authors: Rashmi Chawla, Sunita Dahiya, Gaurav Singh, Giancarlo Fortino
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: This study investigates how heterogeneous information flow in the modular large language model (LLM) pipeline architectures influences hallucination behavior in multidocument and multimodal analysis tasks. Past research in this subject focused on building solutions by consolidating documents first or processing them separately; however, measuring the architectural effect was missing. This research work examines how different LLM architecture pipelines impact hallucination behavior when dealing with multiple heterogeneous documents. To enable a rigorous comparison of downstream reasoning strategies, we introduce an explicit unstructured-to-structured transformation layer incorporating a data stratification framework (DSF) that categorizes heterogeneous inputs into standardized structural classes and applies modality-specific preprocessing for temporal, spatial, visual, and dense narrative data. The experiments are conducted on approximately 3400 multimodal data files across multiple financial industry sectors and educational documents. The results exhibit that parallel extraction with reconciliation consistently achieves the lowest hallucination rates, reducing errors by up to 33% relative to baseline pipelines and yielding the lowest normalized semantic entropy (0.38). The findings are compared with state-of-the-art retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) model used by the Fin-tech industry and further evaluated by human experts, demonstrating that hallucination behavior is strongly shaped by pipeline structure rather than model scale alone. Industry validation reveals that pipeline 3 (parallel extraction + reconciliation) achieves a hallucination score of 0.112, compared to the industry production RAG system (0.135). The results achieved through systematic architectural comparison substantiate that pipeline design can independently determine reliability even when base models are held constant.

title: Automated and Near-Flawless Micropipette PIH Assembly to MEMS Nanoforce Sensor With A Robotic Microscopy System_supp6-3672876.mp4
authors: Wendi Gao
publish: N/A
abstract: Peg-in-hole (PIH) assembly of micropipettes to micro-electromechanical system (MEMS) nanoforce sensors can be performed to improve spatial probing resolution and operation space, but their spatial attitude angle deviations and <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small>-axial distances lead to unstable assembly, sensor performance deterioration, and even structural damage. In this article, an automated and near-flawless micropipette PIH assembly method was proposed based on the built robotic microscopy system, to the best of our knowledge, for the first time. Utilizing an ISPR-based method with a mechanical equilibrium model and a sliding mode controller, the planar deflection angle was adjusted below 0.07<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> without being pushed out of FOV. The out-of-plane inclination angle was then reduced to below 0.05<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> by the proposed GTC-based method, whose depth is obtained from a feasible FDTBR inclination metrology and mechanical contact model. The <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small>-axial distance was actively adjusted by an AIHM-based recenter method during sensor probe insertion, leading to a high assembly successful rate of 100% with negligible contact forces of 1.64 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\boldsymbol{\mu}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>N. The methodology effectiveness was validated through calibration tests, wherein no structural damage and performance deterioration were produced on the processed sensor for a long-term usage, indicating its great potential in nanoforce measurement.

title: Low Profile Multi-Order Reflective Polarization Rotator
authors: Saeed Issa, Abdulrahman Mohammed Alnour, Ali T. Alreshaid, Ahmed Abdelmottaleb Omar
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This work presents a simple methodology for multi-order reflective polarization rotators (RPRs), enabling controllable order response without added unit cell complexity. The approach relies on manipulating current distributions and coupling between multiple resonators to convert linearly polarized incident waves into cross-polarized reflection. The structure comprises three metallic layers separated by two substrates, with a square loop resonator on the top layer and wrapped microstrip lines in the middle layer. The top and middle layers are connected with via holes. Higher-order responses are realized by introducing additional resonators in the middle layer, each is coupled to generate the high-order response. A basic second-order RPR design is extended to a third-order response utilizing two techniques. A fourth-order design is then accomplished by combining the two design techniques employed for the third order response. The third-order designs achieved an effective working band in the regions 4.87-5.56 GHz for the first design and 4.44-5.39 GHz for the second design. While the fourth-order design is operating between 4.43 GHz and 5.3 GHz, which represents a fractional bandwidth of 17.9%. Detailed analysis of the proposed designs is included. A fabricated fourth-order prototype is measured to validate the simulated results. The experimental results show strong agreement with the simulations.

title: Phase-Controlled Magnetic Field Distribution in Halbach-Configured Magnetoelectric Antenna Arrays
authors: Biao Dong, Zhongming Yan, Tianhao Han, Yulong Liu, Youqiang Wu, Hongcheng Zhou, Yu Wang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Magnetoelectric (ME) antennas exhibit unique advantages in low-frequency electromagnetic (EM) applications by virtue of strain-mediated ME coupling, yet the fixed magnetic field distribution severely limits their adaptability to reconfigurable systems. Here, we present a novel ME antenna array that enables dynamic radiation direction manipulation by integrating phase modulation with Halbach topology. The physical correlation between the excitation phase and radiated magnetic field direction of the ME antenna unit is established, and the feasibility of reconstructing the array magnetic field distribution through finite element simulation and experimental measurement is verified. By implementing phase modulation within a Halbach array configuration, either a 1.72-fold amplification of unidirectional magnetic field strength or a ±45° directional shift in magnetic field distribution was demonstrated. This work clarifies the phase-controlled radiation mechanism of ME antenna arrays and provides a new paradigm for the design of reconfigurable ME antenna arrays.

title: Coordinated Differential Protection for Multimode Operation in a Future Spherical Tokamak Fusion Power Plant
authors: Shiyao Yu, Min Zhang, Qiteng Hong, Eduardo Sato, Ioannis Antoniou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: Commercial deployment of the Spherical Tokamak for Energy Production (STEP) requires a reliable protection philosophy for its prototype power plant to prevent system faults from damaging reactor-related components. However, relay protection for the STEP prototype power plant (SPP) microgrid has not yet been specifically addressed. This article develops a coordinated differential protection scheme tailored to the SPP’s multimode operation and hybrid sources. The scheme augments slope-biased current differential protection with a phase-angle-superimposed term derived from two-terminal phasors and employs current restraint for inrush-related disturbances and time synchronization mismatch. Mode-dependent bias curves and pickup thresholds are used to accommodate bidirectional power flow and changing fault levels. The scheme is evaluated on an RTDS/RSCAD model based on U.K. Fusion Energy (UKFE) SPP data, U.K. Grid Code provisions, and IAEA Safety Standards No. SSG-34 credible fault scenarios, covering multiple fault types, locations, impedances, and operating modes. Compared with conventional slope-biased differential protection, the proposed scheme improves the detection of high-impedance internal faults while maintaining external-fault restraint. These results address a protection gap for fusion power plants and support the grid-facing viability of STEP.

title: Deep Manifold Learning for 5G Positioning: Fusing Spatiotemporal Features via Channel Charting
authors: Jiyu Jiao, Gang Wang, Rui Deng, Yuhua Huang, Xiaojun Wang, Jianping Zhu, Yuchen Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: With the integration of 5G and IoT, high-precision indoor positioning is critical for industrial automation, yet traditional methods face challenges like multipath effects and high annotation costs. To address these challenges, we propose a Channel Charting framework based on Siamese Neural Networks (SNN), which learns the low-dimensional manifold structure of channel features to break positioning bottlenecks. A Triple-Stage Adaptive (TSA) filter is first applied to efficiently mitigate multipath-induced signal degradation and enhance robustness. Subsequently, a Time-Angle Delay Channel Frequency Amplitude (T-ADCFA) matrix is designed to decouple time-varying spatial signatures, significantly optimizing discriminability and reducing computational complexity. By leveraging contrastive learning, the proposed framework constructs a discriminative Channel Charting that enables accurate localization with limited supervision. Experimental results demonstrate that the system achieves a 16.7% reduction in late-stage training error in dense rich scattering environments and attains an average positioning error of 6.38 m with significantly fewer labeled samples.

title: Physics–Causality Coupled Residual Graph Attention Networks (PCCResGAT) for Anomaly Detection and Root-Cause Localization in Distribution Grids
authors: Rui Zhang, Ting Lei, Jiayi Liu, Jianli Zhao, Guilin Wang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Diagnosing abnormal events and localizing their sources in distribution grids are complicated by DER-driven operating variability, limited and heterogeneous sensing, and long-tail anomaly distributions. This paper presents PCCResGAT, a physics–causality coupled residual graph-attention framework for unified, node-level anomaly detection, anomaly-type classification, followed by event-wise, propagation-aware root-cause localization. From multivariate electrical measurements, we construct (i) an admittance-derived physics graph that encodes network constraints and (ii) a data-driven temporal causal graph that captures directed influence and time-lag relations among nodes. These two graphs are fused by an adaptive gated residual GAT backbone to obtain topology- and causality-aware representations for diagnosis. For root-cause localization, we perform Top-K ranking using a propagation representation together with a multi-factor scoring rule that integrates causal strength, temporal precedence, and physical-impact cues. An imbalance-aware training objective and calibrated decision rules further improve robustness under long-tail distribution conditions. Experiments on a physics-consistent SimBench-based benchmark with injected anomalies (overload, non-technical-loss/theft, and reactive interference) show that PCCResGAT consistently improves detection and classification performance while yielding more actionable root-cause rankings across diverse operating conditions. Notably, despite inherent measurement noise and stochastic load fluctuations in the real-world data, the proposed framework maintains high robustness.

title: A Bitmap-Guided Collision Tree Anti-collision Protocol for Large-Scale RFID Tags Identification_supp4-3696454.pdf
authors: Xiaolin Jia
publish: N/A
abstract: During the collision resolution phase, many deterministic RFID anti-collision protocols require tags to repeatedly transmit identical ID segments across multiple query cycles. This redundant transmission leads to high communication overhead, increasing identification latency and energy consumption, thereby constraining system performance and scalability. This paper proposes the Bitmap-Guided Collision Tree (BGCT) protocol to address this limitation. BGCT reduces tag transmissions through a progressive information acquisition mechanism, and enables multi-way collision resolution by integrating vector-based grouping with a bitmap. Theoretical analysis and simulation results demonstrate BGCT’s performance advantages. Specifically, for identifying 10000 tags(ID=96 bits), BGCT reduces the total identification time by 27.8% compared to the best competitor in this scenario, EMDT. For 256-bit IDs, assuming uniform or pseudo-random ID distributions, the progressive mechanism mitigates the dependency of communication cost on ID length, maintaining a low expected overhead in contrast to the linear growth observed in other protocols. Furthermore, under non-uniform ID distributions, BGCT demonstrates a collision resolution efficiency 152.9% higher than that of the competing protocols.

title: SPECTRA-AVQA-Net: Sparse Perceptual Enhancement With Cross-Modal Transformation for Audio&#x02013;Visual Question Answering_supp1-3694468.pdf
authors: Debashis Das
publish: N/A

title: Dynamic State-of-Charge Balancing With Hot-Swap Capability in a Modular Energy Storage System Based on Second-Life Batteries
authors: Sajad Rostami, Mohammad Reza Mohammadi, S. Ali Khajehoddin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article presents a fully modular battery equalizer system (MBES) with dynamic state-of-charge (SOC) balancing and hot-swap capability, enabling seamless module replacement without system interruption. Each battery module (BM) connects to a battery equalizer unit (BEU) that provides autonomous control, protection, and measurement while sharing SOC data via distributed communication synchronized by a low-frequency clock. A simple control algorithm governs module insertion and bypassing decisions, enabling rapid SOC equalization even under severe capacity mismatches. High-fidelity simulations show fast convergence across 10 series-connected heterogeneous BMs with 33%–100% initial SOC variation. Experimental results from a 22.5 kW, 450 V prototype confirm balancing times of 54–66 min for modules ranging from 40–100 Ah. The MBES extends system lifespan up to 85% higher than conventional system and reduces cost per end-of-life (EOL) cycle by 38%, offering a practical, scalable solution for second-life battery (SLB) applications with improved balancing speed, fault resilience, and economic performance.

title: Cardan–Worm Bimodal Actuator for Underactuated Walking and Running With Soft Hip Exoskeleton
authors: Luka Miskovic, Enrica Tricomi, Vinzenz Gerhard, Alex Nanni, Federico Masiero, Jacob Urban, Luca Palmerini, Lorenzo Chiari, Lorenzo Masia
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Actuators in lower-limb wearable robots must frequently reverse rotation direction due to the cyclic back-and-forth motion of human gait. Such reversals impair high-frequency tracking and induce current spikes, raising energy consumption through repeated deceleration and re-acceleration of the attached load. This effect intensifies with the number of joints and actuators, which can be mitigated through underactuation; although motion reversal then becomes an even greater problem for high-frequency command tracking. In this work, we design and validate a novel actuator with a Cardan–Worm gear mechanism for an underactuated hip exosuit, operating in bidirectional (reversing) mode at lower frequencies characteristic of walking and switching to unidirectional (spinning) mode at higher frequencies typical of running. Experiments showed that reversing mode struggles with high-frequency commands, whereas spinning mode achieves precise tracking and reduces energy consumption by 21.27%. Further experiments with six healthy subjects wearing the hip exosuit demonstrated that the adaptive controller successfully switches between walking and running and changes the actuation mode between reversing and spinning accordingly. A high mean Pearson's correlation between leg and actuator output (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.95 \pm 0.015$</tex-math></inline-formula>) and magnitude-squared coherence in walking (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.98 \pm 0.0033$</tex-math></inline-formula>) and running (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.98 \pm 0.0013$</tex-math></inline-formula>) confirm the actuator accurately tracks the reference during assisted use.

title: Reliable Interpretations of Deep Learning-based Malware Detectors via Deep Q-Networks_supp1-3699408.pdf
authors: Yuan Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Deep learning has become widely used in Android malware detection, but its black-box nature raises trust concerns, limiting its use in critical security areas. To address this, various interpretation methods have been proposed. Unfortunately, these solutions often suffer from inconsistent results and poor adaptability to model updates. In this work, we propose XDQNMal, a Deep Q-Networks (DQN)-based global interpretation framework designed to uncover the critical features that drive decisions in deep learning-based malware detectors. To enhance the reliability of interpretation, XDQNMal captures API call frequency features derived from the runtime behavior of each application (App). Then, it unites a DQN model with the TabPFN detection model to work collaboratively, using variations in detection results as reward signals. These signals guide the DQN model to gradually identify the most impactful features as interpretations for the detection model’s decisions. Our experimental evaluation on real-world datasets demonstrates that the proposed XDQNMal framework generates reliable interpretation for deep learning-based malware detection models. For instance, suppressing the critical features identified by XDQNMal leads to an average decrease of 20.30% in the probability that the malicious sample is predicted as malicious, highlighting the pivotal role these features play in the model’s decision-making.

title: One Text, Five Years, Multiple Systems: A Longitudinal Case Study of French-Czech Machine Translation (2020-2025)
authors: Kateřina Motalík Hodková
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: In this paper, we examine French-Czech machine translation produced by widely used online systems (Google Translate, DeepL, Bing Translator) and by a large language model (GPT). Since 2020, we have collected translations of the same short journalistic text, resulting in a corpus of 29 translations created over a five-year period. This controlled setup allows us to observe how individual systems evolve over time with a constant source text. The translations were evaluated from two complementary perspectives. First, two groups of human evaluators (11 participants in total) assessed each version using four criteria: fluency, adequacy, lexical choice, and style. Second, we identified 14 segments of the source text that repeatedly proved difficult to translate and examined how these passages were handled across systems and over time. The combination of longitudinal observation with both global scoring and focused linguistic analysis offers a detailed view of the development of translation quality of several publicly available MT systems.

title: Machine Learning-Based Path Loss Modeling for mmWave Wireless Communication Systems: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Samba Siva Reddy Mula, Vinay Kumar Pamula
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Accurate path loss modeling is a fundamental requirement for the design, optimization, and deployment of millimeter-wave (mmWave) and beyond wireless communication systems. Traditional empirical and deterministic models, while well-established, often struggle to capture the highly dynamic, environment-dependent propagation characteristics encountered in dense urban, indoor, and mobility-driven scenarios. In recent years, machine learning (ML)-based approaches have emerged as promising alternatives due to their ability to learn complex nonlinear relationships from measurement data. This paper presents a comprehensive and systematic survey of ML-driven path loss modeling techniques for mmWave communications, covering classical ML, deep learning, hybrid physics-informed, and data-efficient learning paradigms. A structured taxonomy is developed to categorize existing methods based on learning architecture, input features, environmental adaptability, and computational complexity. Furthermore, a comparative analysis is conducted to highlight performance trends, generalization capability across environments and frequencies, interpretability, and deployment feasibility. By synthesizing recent advances and identifying key research challenges-such as data scarcity, crossenvironment transferability, and model explainability-this survey provides practical insights and future research directions toward scalable and intelligent channel modeling for next-generation and 6G wireless networks.

title: Federated Variational Multi-Modal Transformer for Privacy-Preserving Disease Prognosis in Edge-Deployed IoMT Networks
authors: R. Vinoth Kumar, A. Selvakumar
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: The Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) has grown extremely fast and offers more opportunities than ever to proactive and data-driven healthcare, but the sheer variety of multimodal data and strict privacy demands are considered to be a big challenge. In this paper, to enhance privacy of patients, FedVarMMT, a Federated Variational Multi-Modal Transformer based framework that combines heterogeneous data consisting of physiological signals, medical images, and electronic health records, will be presented. The framework uses modality-specific Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) to learn latent representations that are probabilistic and resistant to missing or incomplete information and uses federation cross-attention to learn inter-modal dependencies between distributed edge devices without exchanging raw patient information. FedVarMMT is optimized to make inferences with low latency, it is capable of using dynamic modality dropout and quantized attention, which allows timely making accurate prognosis on the resource-constrained hardware. The experiments conducted on diabetes and cardiovascular data indicate 94.8 percent of the accuracy, 96.2 percent of the AUC, and 40 percent of latency reduction in comparison to centralized fusion, but guarantee strong, privacy-conscience predictions. These results demonstrate the possible potential of FedVarMMT to revolutionize the process of delivering healthcare in a distributed manner that will enhance the level of accessibility, security, and equity of monitoring a patient.

title: The Role of User Emotional Expression and Semantic Cues in Improving Content Recommendation Accuracy and Their Data Feature Analysis
authors: Xiaoyu Gu
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 International Conference on AI Decision-Making and Management
abstract: None

title: The Evolving Battlefield: A Data-Driven Machine Learning Method for Identifying and Alleviating Contemporary DDoS Attacks
authors: Priyaj Sony, Ebtasam Ahmad Siddiqui, Uday Pratap Singh, Bersha Kumari
publish: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Information Management & Machine Intelligence
abstract: None

title: Advancing a Sustainable Future: Integrating Technological Innovation, Policy, and Community Engagement: A Review
authors: Mohammad Ghulam Ali
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: There is a growing global crisis concerning sustainability across scientific, economic, and policy domains, linked to the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Achieving a sustainable future requires awareness of the UNSDGs and coordinated efforts from education, commerce, NonGovernmental Organizations (NGOs), government bodies and individuals with clear guidelines and policy frameworks for generating awareness and then through interdisciplinary research and enforcement of important initiatives on the major issues such as responsibly using resources, engaging with ecofriendly innovations, renewable energy, green initiatives, ethical business practices, climate change and reducing environmental impact and environmental protection, social equity, sustainable agriculture, food security, clean water, waste management, urban development, and economic resilience to achieve economic development and to ensure long-term well-being and to create a healthier world governance system. This literature review examines current research in Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) on sustainability and its promotion through interdisciplinary education. Analyzing the synergy between technological innovation, policy frameworks, and community actions, the paper proposes an inclusive interdisciplinary framework to integrate with the UN-SDGs goals and achievements. It includes a research indicators framework to measure progress and discusses some case studies demonstrating the benefits of interdependent approaches. The necessity of applying sustainability research beyond academia is emphasized, with the goal of fostering a more equitable, participatory, and adaptable global society. Advancing a sustainable future means making choices today that protect the planet for future generations.

title: NEXUS: A Microservice-Based Network Emulation Platform with Topology Isolation, Flow-Based Analytics, and AI-Driven Control
authors: Ege Erberk Uslu, Orhan Dagdeviren
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Network emulation platforms constitute fundamental infrastructure for prototyping and evaluating distributed systems, routing protocols, Software-Defined Networking (SDN) control logic, and network security mechanisms. However, existing emulation workflows encounter significant challenges when experiments require frequent runtime topology modifications, heterogeneous protocol stacks, flow-based analytics with robust observability, and integration with NFV Management and Orchestration (MANO) frameworks. This paper presents NEXUS (Network EXperimentation Unified System), a microservicebased network emulation platform that decouples the privileged emulation engine from a containerized control plane. The platform persists topologies as database-backed constructs, instantiates per-topology emulation containers on demand, and exposes unified control through an API gateway. Our architecture, comprising 21 microservices, supports concurrent execution of multiple isolated topologies, CRIU-based checkpoint/restore operations, and per-topology observability stacks. We systematically identify 19 functionalities for network emulation platforms and demonstrate through comparative analysis that NEXUS is, to the best of our knowledge, the first system to consolidate all of them, including nine advanced functionalities F11 through F19 that have not been consolidated in a single emulation platform in prior work. We additionally report a single-host experimental evaluation measuring topology instantiation up to 100 nodes in 32.06 s, runtime modification latency of 91.5 ms for link addition and 260.8 ms for node addition, and concurrent scalability limits that motivate future distributed deployment.

title: AI based Facial Recognition for Smart Access Control and Identity Validation
authors: Sowjanya Addu, Sruthi Pulipati, Rupa Venkata Laxmi Bathini, Sathwika Puram, Sai Rishitha Kayala, Leyha Dandyala
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: In today’s digital world, secure authentication is essential to protect systems and user data from unauthorized access. Traditional methods such as Passwords, PINs, ID cards, OTPs, and Multi-Factor Authentication are widely used but they remain vulnerable to theft, impersonation, unauthorized access, and malpractices. In high-security environments, manual identity verification is time-consuming and can prone to human error. To overcome these challenges, this project proposes an AI-based facial recognition system for smart and secure access control for accurate identity validation. The system allows users to register their facial data, which is converted into unique facial embeddings and stored in a database. The proposed model utilizes the InsightFace deep learning framework for face detection and face recognition, enabling accurate and efficient identity verification. Extracted facial embeddings are compared with stored feature vectors to determine user authorization, and access is granted or denied accordingly. Overall, this AI-driven system offers a secure, fast, and contactless authentication solution suitable for offices, educational institutions, and other secure digital environments. By leveraging deep learning and computer vision techniques, the proposed approach strengthens security, reduces reliance on manual verification, and addresses many of the weaknesses associated with conventional authentication methods.

title: Deep Learning-Based Quantitative Coral Growth and Bleaching Assessment, Thermal Forecasting, and GIS-Based Coral Nursery Placement Planning
authors: Dinithi Wickramaarachchi, Sahan Kavishka, Senith Udara, Gimhani Brahmanage, Samitha Vidhanaarachchi, Thilini Jayalath, Hansi De Silva
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Coral reef restoration approaches require quantitative monitoring of coral growth and bleaching severity. Existing thermal forecasting models are constrained to the ocean surface layer, leaving colony-level growth and bleaching severity unquantified and subsurface thermal stress undetected. This study presents an integrated, field-validated monitoring approach for active reef restoration sites, evaluated at Port City, Colombo, Sri Lanka. Instance segmentation using YOLO11 was applied to detect coral colonies, with geometric calibration converting pixellevel masks into absolute surface area measurements for longitudinal growth tracking. Transfer-learning CNNs were evaluated for species classification on segmented corals, and three methods were compared for quantifying bleaching severity at the individual colony level. A depth-stratified CNN-LSTM architecture was developed to forecast subsurface ocean temperatures across 0-10 m depth bands, extending beyond the 0-1 m surface layer addressed by existing models, with thermal anomaly detection integrated as a heat-stress alert mechanism. A GIS-based multicriteria approach using the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) was applied to rank 300 candidate nursery locations using insitu sensor data and spatial attributes. YOLO11 achieved a mask mAP @ 0.5 of 0.829, EfficientNet-B0 achieved a species classification accuracy of 9 0. 4 4%, geometric calibration yielded a mean area error of 1. 1 5%, and the CNN-LSTM thermal model achieved a composite R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.79 across depth layers. The AHP approach achieved an 80% expert agreement rate. These results demonstrate the feasibility of a quantitative, multi-task, ecologically grounded monitoring pipeline under real-world field conditions.

title: A Method for Source to Object Code Traceability Analysis in Model-Based Development
authors: Lili Chen, Siqi Wu, Zhongtong Huang, Jinghua Sun
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Software Engineering and Computer Science (CSECS)
abstract: In the safety-critical domain of civil aviation, Level A software must satisfy the objective of verifying additional object code that cannot be directly traced to source code, as mandated by RTCA DO-178C and DO-331. However, existing traceability analysis research typically relies on representative subsets of manually developed code in accordance with coding standards. This differs significantly from the characteristics of auto-generated code in Model-Based Development (MBD), which is produced by code generators strictly following tool specifications and templates, exhibiting deterministic structures. The distinctive style of such generated code cannot be replicated by manually following coding standards, rendering conventional traceability methods inadequate in this context. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a traceability analysis method based on a model feature set, elevating the traceability evidence from the code level to the model level. By parsing the modeling specifications, the method defines a comprehensive feature set covering typical airborne software modeling elements across functional and complexity dimensions, thereby constructing traceability paths from model to object code. The paper elaborates on the construction of the model feature set and the traceability analysis process, and demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of the method through a case study of an avionics system. The proposed approach has been successfully applied in the airworthiness compliance practice for Level A airborne software, with a model scale corresponding to approximately 60,000 lines of generated code. Results show that the proposed feature set can comprehensively cover the model content and effectively satisfies source-to-object-code traceability requirements mandated by DO-178C Objective A7-9 and DO-331 Objective MB7-9 for Level A software, providing a practicable approach for traceability verification in MBD processes.

title: On the Fundamental Limitations of Neural Networks for Statistical Randomness Testing: A Negative Result
authors: Oğuzhan Durmuş, Gökhan Dalkılıç
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The use of neural networks for testing random number generators has been proposed as a potentially better alternative to traditional test suites such as National Institute of Standards and Technology Special Publications (NIST SP) 800-22. This paper presents a negative result, demonstrating through both theoretical analysis and empirical investigation that neural network-based approaches face fundamental barriers that prevent them from serving as real replacements for established statistical tests. We identify three critical limitations: (1) the huge sample space problem, where sequences of n bits create a space of 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">n</sup> possibilities that no practical training dataset can represent; (2)information loss when converting continuous p-values in traditional tests to binary fail or pass classifications; and (3) a misalignment between the proposed Internet of Things deployment scenario and actual real-world randomness testing workflows. We implemented state of the art transformer-based architectures for our experiments and critically analyzed recent works proposing small neural networks for individual NIST tests. These reference methodologies, which claim high accuracy, exhibit fundamental flaws, including circular labeling, single-source training data, and the absence of generalization testing. Our own experiments with transformer-based architectures achieving high accuracy on heldout test sets seem successful, but they fail for cryptographic applications. We discuss why seemingly positive experimental results can be misleading and offer recommendations for future research directions in this area.

title: An IoT–WSN–Machine Learning and Cloud Integrated Framework for Real-Time Smart Irrigation and Crop Health Monitoring
authors: Mahesh Recharla, M. Charles Arockiaraj, D. Kamalakkannan, Sowmiya M, Prathima Gamini
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: The management of agricultural water resources and the problem of water shortage are pressing global concerns. Water is wasted, money is wasted, and the ecosystem is affected by traditional irrigation practices like improper scheduling and overwatering. Precision water distribution and crop health monitoring are made possible by smart irrigation systems that use actuators, control valves, and sensors. Irrigation management decisions are improved by cleaning, normalizing, and extracting features from the critical data produced by these sensors. In order to better monitor crop health; this study presents a hybrid LR-RNN model that can detect abnormal sequences of irrigation and plant response data. Unlike traditional approaches, this technology uses machine learning and deep learning to capture intricate patterns of water flow and agricultural well-being. With a 94.50% success rate, the proposed algorithm distinguished between watering conditions that were conducive to healthy growth and those that were either harmful or inefficient. These results show that by using automated and precise irrigation, hybrid intelligence systems improve crop health, save water, and cut down on economic losses.

title: MultiLayer SVM Model for Intelligent Pest Detection and Precision Crop Protection in Smart Agriculture
authors: Hemagowri J, Amsaveni G, S Venkata Vara Prasad, Deepak Asrani, Komal Asrani, Mythili M
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: By integrating biological, chemical, and cultural approaches, Integrated Pest Management (IPM) keeps insect populations at economically harmful levels while lowering hazards to public health and the environment. It is an environmentally friendly and sustainable agricultural strategy. In this study, it applies state-of-the-art methods of ML and image processing to the problem of intelligent pest detection and crop protection as it pertains to smart agriculture. Two pest image datasets with 21 pest categories are used in the study. A Multilayer SVM is used to classify the images after they have undergone segmentation, noise reduction, and PCA-LDA feature extraction. With a 96.21% accuracy rate, the model shows that combining machine learning and pest identification allows for accurate and sustainable crop protection in smart agriculture. The study concludes that smart agricultural approaches can be made more sustainable and effective in protecting crops when machine learning and intelligent pest detection.

title: Secured Image and Audio Communication Over the Wireless Channel Using 5D Chaotic Maps and UNET
authors: Thanikaiselvan V., Meghana K., Suma Varshitha J., Manvitha Reddy M., Subathra S.
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Secure transmission of images is becoming challenge due to risks such as data interception, distortion, and loss of protected information. Several traditional encryption techniques do not remain stable and effective when exposed to channel noise. To address these issues, this paper introduces a modified high-dimensional chaotic encryption approach for secure image transmission. A 5-D chaotic map is applied, and its control parameters are obtained from image features extracted using U-Net and processed through SHA-512 hashing. This approach ensures strong randomness and high key sensitivity. Encryption is performed using multiple rounds of confusion and diffusion to break pixel correlations and improve unpredictability. The encrypted images are added with noisy environments, like Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN), Salt-and-Pepper noise, and random noise, to evaluate channel robustness. During decryption, an inverse chaotic sequence is applied to obtain the original image, even under noise conditions and the results show PSNR values below 35 dB for encrypted images, SSIM values close to 0.98, NPCR reaches 99.60 after decryption.

title: Design of an Low SAR $2 \times 2$ Array Structure for Portable Disaster Relief Devices
authors: Bharath Velu, Vignesh V, Dharani dharan V, T Annalakshmi, S Sharon Sweeti, Vijayakumari G
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: The objective of the proposed patch array (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times 2$</tex>) of 2.4 GHz in the ISM band is designed to improve emergency communications systems in the disaster-relief scenario and the traditional ISM based applications. CST Studio Suite is used to analyze key antenna metrics including S 11, gain, directivity, bandwidth, and radiation efficiency. The results of the simulation indicate that the array offers a better directivity, gain and less return loss than the single-patch structure. The antenna can be used with Wi-Fi, Bluetooth-enabled IoT devices, as well as sensor-based networks, due to its stable radiation behaviour, which provides and efficient and reliable solution to current communication requirements in the ISM- band.

title: Development of an Efficient Wireless Power Transfer Technique for Electric Vehicles
authors: Sandeep Gupta, Pradeep Kumar Tiwari, Anand S, Sandeep Kanaujia
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Wireless power transfer offers an alternative for contactless electric vehicle charging (EV). This technique charges a vehicle progressively, reducing inconvenience while avoiding conductive connectors and cables. Meeting kilowatt power-level efficiencies in excess of 90% remains challenging due to large air gaps between coils, inevitable coil misalignment, coupling-coefficient variation, interoperability, and electromagnetic-exposure constraints. This paper discusses the development of an efficient inductive WPT technique for EV charging based on a double-sided LCC (inductor–capacitor–capacitor) compensation network and a lightweight adaptive frequency strategy. The compensation design is based on a loss-aware resonant model linked to reflected impedance and reactive power oscillation, which reduces current stress and improves the inverter input power factor. The controller proposed in this work tracks the phase angle between the inverter voltage and current to ensure near-zero phase-angle operation, enabling soft switching and immunity to detuning effects arising from component tolerance and alignment variations. Circuit-level simulation is performed for a 7.7 kW class system with realistic parasitic resistances and coupling profiles for lateral misalignment. The findings indicate that the anticipated technology maintains efficiency above 90 percent dc-to-dc as the misalignment rate increases. Moreover, its delivered power is much more even than that of the Series–Series (SS) and Series–Parallel (SP) compensation techniques.

title: Energy-Efficient Air Conditioner Placement Optimization using Genetic Algorithm
authors: A. Rehana, G.Anuradha, B Venkateswara Rao, T. Vishnu, Sk. Sameer
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Energy-efficient cooling in indoor environments remains a critical challenge due to uneven thermal distribution and increasing power consumption. An optimization framework based on a Genetic Algorithm (GA) is proposed to determine the optimal placement of air conditioning (AC) units within enclosed spaces. The approach enhances thermal uniformity, minimizes temperature variance, and reduces energy usage through intelligent spatial optimization. Evolutionary computation mechanisms including population-based search, fitness evaluation, selection, crossover, and adaptive mutation are employed to identify near-optimal AC positions within a predefined room layout. The optimization model incorporates cooling coverage, distance-based thermal influence, airflow constraints, and power efficiency considerations to ensure balanced environmental comfort. Simulation results indicate that GA-based placement significantly improves cooling uniformity and energy efficiency compared to conventional manual or random positioning strategies. The results demonstrate that evolutionary optimization techniques offer a scalable and effective solution for intelligent indoor climate management.

title: Analyzing the Synergy Between CNN Feature Extraction and RL Policy Optimization in Autonomous Vehicle Environment
authors: R. Poorvadevi
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: A key component of contemporary intelligent systems, machine learning (ML) enables data-driven automation and decision-making in a variety of fields. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are a particularly effective machine learning technique for computer vision tasks because of their remarkable ability to automatically extract meaningful features from raw visual data. Simultaneously, Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers an effective framework for continuously interacting with an environment to learn the best decision-making techniques. In the context of autonomous driving systems, this paper explores the synergy between CNN-based feature extraction and RL-inspired decision optimization. A CNN-based end-to-end driving model is put into practice and assessed in a self-driving simulation. Robust perception, real-time control, and adaptability to difficult driving situations like shifting lighting and shadowed road segments are the main goals of the suggested method. To increase generalization, extensive data augmentation is used, such as shadow simulation and traditional vision-based feature enhancement. The effectiveness of CNN-driven perception pipelines for autonomous vehicle applications is demonstrated by experimental results, which show that the suggested system achieves stable convergence, robust steering control, and successful navigation across multiple simulated tracks.

title: Modeling and Optimization of Noise-Tolerant Coding Algorithms for 6G-oriented Communication
authors: Sergii Dunaiev, Stanislav Milevskyi, Iryna Aksonova, Zhyhalov Mykhailo, Oleksandr Pasko, Ruslan Trofymenko
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The relevance of the study is due to the transition to 6G-oriented networks, which are characterized by ultra-high data rates, minimal delays, and operation in heterogeneous and unstable channels. Under such conditions, traditional methods of noise-tolerant coding lose their effectiveness, which requires adaptive and self-tuning information recovery algorithms. The article analyzes the impact of random, impulse and packet interference and packet erasures on transmission reliability. The characteristics of LT-, LDPC- and Raptor codes are compared, and the conditions for using maximum likelihood decoding for fountain codes are also studied. Architectural models of fountain coding systems are proposed and a step-bystep simulation model for evaluating performance is developed, on the basis of which the Raptor coding algorithm is improved. The proposed simulation framework establishes a theoretical basis for evaluating the probability of message recovery, redundancy, and transmission delay compared to classical schemes. Preliminary analytical evaluation suggests the potential effectiveness of the optimized fountain and LDPC algorithms for highly reliable 6 G networks, necessitating further comprehensive numerical validation.

title: Spectroscopy-Based Automated Ripeness Estimation of Banana Varieties
authors: B. Dhivya, J. Raja
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Global food security is closely tied to agriculture, where accurate fruit ripeness assessment is essential for minimizing post-harvest losses and maximizing market value. Bananas, being a major globally traded fruit, demand precise ripeness evaluation to ensure quality control and supply chain efficiency. Post-harvest losses in bananas can reach up to 3040 % due to improper ripeness detection, leading to significant economic and resource waste. Traditional methods like checking products by hand or using chemicals is slow, stressful on the workers, and can't always tell you the same thing. Some machine vision techniques can help make things a bit better, but they still don't work well in darker places, can only look at the surface, and aren't as accurate as humans are. To overcome these limitations, this study suggest using a simple and nondestructive method called deep learning with hyperspectral images and spectroscopy to check how ripe fruit is, in real time. The system uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to find important patterns in the fruit pictures and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to analyze the sequential data from different ripening stages, which helps group the fruits into their proper categories, no matter how much they change as they get riper. The experimental results show that the new model was able to classify data with an accuracy of over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 9. 5 \%}$</tex>. This helps farmers decide how and when to harvest, which means there are fewer spoiled crops and a better quality of food all the way to stores or restaurants.

title: An Empirical Evaluation of Machine Learning Techniques for Return Prediction using NIFTY-50 Weekly Data
authors: Vinod Jain
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Accurately forecasting stock market returns is widely recognized as a complex challenge in financial analytics, primarily due to the stochastic nature, non-linear patterns, and pronounced volatility inherent in financial markets. The growing accessibility of historical market data, combined with significant advances in computational intelligence, has positioned machine learning (ML) approaches as effective alternatives to conventional econometric techniques. This study conducts a detailed empirical assessment of various machine learning models for forecasting weekly returns of NIFTY-50 stocks using three years of historical data. Both linear and non-linear learning algorithms are examined, including Linear Regression, Ridge Regression, Support Vector Regression (SVR), Random Forest, Extra Trees, AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, and CatBoost. Weekly OHLCV (Open, High, Low, Close, and Volume) data are utilized to mitigate short-term market noise and improve temporal consistency. Model performance is assessed using Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and the R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score. The experimental findings indicate that linear models and ensemble-based tree methods consistently outperform more complex non-linear approaches for short-term weekly return prediction, whereas SVR exhibits a notable decline in performance. These results underscore the inherent difficulty of equity return forecasting and highlight the importance of developing hybrid and regime-aware predictive frameworks. The study offers meaningful insights for researchers and practitioners engaged in quantitative finance and portfolio optimization.

title: Two-Way Communication System Using Dynamic ISL Gesture Recognition and Voice Processing
authors: M. Vijay Kumar, Ramamohana Rao Gudaru, K. Sravya, Bhanu Chander Balusa, N. Sai Praneetha, B. Thrinadh
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Communication in sign language and normal language remains to be a big challenge for hearing-impaired and normally abled citizens due to the existing differences in both sign language and normal language communication. Most works on this problem still exist for one-way communication. On that ground, this paper shall propose a system allowing for real-time two-way communication through voice processing in reliance upon dynamic Indian Sign Language gesture recognition. This will, perhaps, ensure effective communication. It employs a webcam in capturing hand gestures. It also relies on recognizing dynamic sign language gestures corresponding to highly popular words. This study, therefore, uses a deep learning algorithm that employs a vision transformer (VIT) in the training of a preprocessed dataset containing almost two thousand classes of word-level gestures. For the purpose of attaining significant communication in this work, natural language aspects and tokenizers shall be employed to compose complete sentences corresponding to every word recognition in the interface. These are applied for the use of text and speech generation, whereas the feedback communication channel is applied for the use of user input, whether typing or speech inputs, allotted for the recognition of the gesture in the form of the gesture videos for the signing process under the context of Sign Language. The experimental results effectively show that the proposed system for gesture recognition is functional at an accurate level of 98%, thereby ensuring efficiency, reliability, and quicker processing speed, since the processes involved are real-time. The two-way communication channel can be effectively implemented without the aid of any hardware components. Finally, the proposed system is reliable, efficient, and a pleasant experience in the context of Sign Language for Education, Sign Language for Health, or Sign Language for Service.

title: Machine Learning Framework for Crop Yield Prediction under Climate Variability
authors: B.Thulasi, R Varaprasad, Karanam Poorna Srikala, Kamarthi Swapna, Boya Akhilandeswari, Chakali Anjali
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Climate variability, extreme events, and complex climate–crop interactions increasingly challenge reliable crop yield prediction. Most existing machine learning approaches rely on coarse climatic averages and ignore growth-stage-specific stress, limiting robustness and interpretability. To address this, a climate-variability-aware framework is proposed using event-based stress descriptors, growth-stage aggregation, and XGBoost regression. Daily ERA5 reanalysis data are transformed into indicators of heat stress, extreme rainfall, dry-spell persistence, and standardized drought (SPEI), which are aggregated across early, mid, and late crop development stages. These features are used to train XGBoost models with cross-year validation, while SHAP analysis provides interpretability of climate impacts. Evaluation using CY-Bench yield data achieves strong performance (MAE = 0.42, RMSE = 0.58, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.95), outperforming Random Forest, Support Vector Regression, and Deep Neural Networks. Climate variability descriptors reduce RMSE by about 25% compared to annual means, and SPEI improves drought-year robustness. Results consistently identify mid-stage heat stress and late-stage drought as dominant yield drivers. Overall, explicit modelling of climate variability and growth-stage sensitivity enhances accuracy, robustness, and interpretability, offering a reproducible and climate-conscious solution for agricultural decision support.

title: Comparative Analysis of DC–DC Converter Topologies for Portable Solar-Powered EV Charging Systems
authors: Vineeth Kumar P K, Devika S, Jijesh J J, Ramya P, Manju D, Lakshmi Manasa B
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: The Electric Vehicle (EV) charging facility is a challenge in remote areas where the traditional grid facility is limited. To overcome the issues of limited availability of grid facility, the Portable EV system is becoming attractive in the mobility sector. The Portable EV charging system is powered by the Solar Photovoltaic Panel (SPV). The power electronic DC-DC converter is the heart of the portable EV charging system. In this paper, a comparative analysis of suitable DC-DC converters for a portable EV charging system is presented. Each DC-DC converter is modelled in the MATLAB/Simulink environment, and the results were analyzed under different conditions. The following performance parameters, such as power conversion efficiency, presence of ripple content, and dynamic responses, are considered to evaluate the performance. The simulation results show that the interleaved buck-boost converter topology is a more viable option for a portable EV charging system. The power conversion ratio of the interleaved buck-boost converter is 94.5%, the voltage regulation accuracy of 95%, and the reduced system downtime is 24s. Also, the interleaved buck-boost converter dominates over traditional DC-DC converters in terms of fault detection time, fault isolation time, and fault prediction time. The proposed research gives an idea about the selection of DC-DC converters for a portable EV charging system.

title: Smart Agri-Suite: A Globally Deployable Machine Learning Platform for Satellite-Driven Idle Land Mobilization and Smallholder Agricultural Decision Support
authors: Bowatte W M E, Maheepala S A D J N, T C N De Silva, Rupasinghe I D S D, Dhammika H. De Silva, Abeywardhana D L
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Across the tropics, 500 million smallholder farmers drive global food security. However, they face three connected, systemic failures. First, fertile land often remains undetected and idle. Second, market transactions lack legal or technical safeguards. Third, farmers make critical pricing, inventory, and labor decisions without reliable data. These challenges are not isolated inefficiencies. Instead, they act as planetary-scale barriers to agricultural prosperity. This study introduces the Smart Agri-Suite. It is a modular, mobile-first platform designed to address these exact barriers. The system integrates satellite intelligence, machine learning, and digital governance. Together, these form a comprehensive, field-ready solution. The platform processes Sentinel-2 Level-2A imagery using an XGBoost classifier. This enables precise, parcel-level land mapping and crop suitability analysis. Consequently, the system identifies previously undetected arable land with 80% accuracy. The platform features a compliance-driven marketplace. The marketplace strictly enforces spatial and legal verification for every transaction. Data-driven pricing and forecasting components incorporate demand signals, transportation costs and Net Present Value. These algorithms support financially sound decision-making. Furthermore, quantile regressionbased inventory management provides probabilistic demand forecasts. It also generates automated stock alerts. The system features a crop-agnostic and language-neutral architecture. This design demonstrates direct transferability to other smallholder systems. It can easily adapt to Sub-Saharan Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and Latin America. These findings highlight the power of targeted technological integration. It can transform persistent agricultural inefficiencies into measurable prosperity. Ultimately, this creates equitable growth for farming communities worldwide.

title: A Novel Technology Towards Carbon Smart Road- Development of Value-Added Products From End-Of-Life Plastic Waste
authors: Mahesh W Kasture, Gagandeep Singh, Ambika Behl, Chiranjeevi T, Ravi Kumar V., Chandrasekhar
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: The disposal of waste plastic remains a major global environmental challenge (1-3). Despite numerous efforts, the complexity of managing unsegregated municipal waste-particularly mixed plastics-continues to hinder the development of sustainable and cost-effective solutions. Due to the lack of appropriate environment-friendly technological solutions, the unmanaged plastic waste is not only threat to the environment but also causes serious health hazards for living beings. Looking at the complexity of mixed waste plastic, a sustainable and economical pathway still eludes the scientific community. Therefore, it is imperative that there is an urgent need to address this issue <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(4,5)$</tex>. Traditional roadbuilding methods-heavily dependent on asphalt, bitumen, and energy-intensive processes-contribute significantly to carbon emissions. In response, the concept of Carbon Smart Roads has emerged as a transformative solution, integrating carbon reduction, resource efficiency, and innovative materials into the very foundation of modern infrastructure (6). One of the cornerstones of Carbon Smart Roads is the use of recycled and alternative materials. Conventional bitumen and aggregates are replaced or supplemented with Recycled plastic waste, turning an environmental challenge into a construction resource. Industrial by-products such as fly ash, steel slag, and waste glass, reducing the need for virgin materials. Bio-binders and warm mix asphalt, which require lower heating temperatures and emit significantly less <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex>. Geocell and geosynthetics technologies that stabilize soil and reduce material consumption while enhancing road longevity. These materials not only reduce emissions but also enhance the durability and resilience of road structures, lowering maintenance requirements and life-cycle costs.

title: An Efficient Hybrid Deep Learning Model for Diabetic Retinopathy Detection Using Fundus Images
authors: Geeta Tripathi, P.Pratima Rani, Vullam Nagagopiraju, Ch.Siva Subrahmanyam, A.Lakshmanarao, P Chandra Sekhar Reddy
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Diabetic retinopathy is a progressive eye disease and a major cause of vision loss among individuals with diabetes, highlighting the need for reliable automated screening systems. Recent advances in deep learning have shown promising results in fundus image analysis; however, performance often depends on the quality and depth of learned feature representations. This paper proposes a deep feature fusion approach for diabetic retinopathy classification using fundus images. The framework first establishes a baseline using a convolutional neural network trained from scratch and then evaluates multiple transfer learning models, including VGG, Inception, and EfficientNet, to learn discriminative retinal features. To further enhance classification performance, multi-level deep features are extracted and fused to integrate complementary information related to retinal texture, lesion patterns, and disease severity. Experiments conducted on a publicly available fundus image dataset demonstrate that transfer learning models outperform the baseline CNN, while the proposed deep feature fusion approach achieves the highest classification accuracy. The results validate the effectiveness of multi-level feature fusion in improving diabetic retinopathy classification from fundus images.

title: Enhanced Doppler-Based River Flow Measurement System Using Cross-Correlation Signal Processing
authors: A.V. Neftissov, A. A. Biloshchytskyi, I. M. Kazambaev, L. N. Kirichenko, A. Medetkhan, A.D. Mikryakov
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: This paper presents an improved Doppler-based flow measurement system that applies cross-correlation analysis to enhance the accuracy of open-channel velocity estimation. The proposed approach minimizes turbulence-induced errors and signal phase instability common in conventional Doppler flow meters. The hardware implementation is based on a Raspberry Pi 4 platform integrating 40 kHz ultrasonic sensors and realtime signal processing modules. Field experiments conducted on the Talas River demonstrated that the traditional Doppler method can deviate from reference hydrometric measurements by up to 10%, whereas the cross-correlation technique reduces the deviation to less than 2%. The system's mathematical model and experimental validation confirm that the correlation-based approach ensures stable, precise, and low-cost flow monitoring suitable for long-term hydrological observation and automated water management systems.

title: Evaluating the Efficacy of AlphaEarth Embeddings for Tree Species Mapping in Complex Terrain
authors: Yingjie Wang, Xiao Zhang, Gui Gao, Liangyun Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate tree species mapping in complex terrain is critical for forest management yet remains hindered by spectral confusion and topographic effects in remote sensing imagery. The emergence of remote sensing foundation models introduces a promising paradigm shift toward “representation learning”. This study systematically evaluates the AlphaEarth Foundation (AEF) model for tree species classification in Sichuan Province, a region characterized by fragmented topography and pronounced vertical vegetation zonation. We designed a comparative framework comprising three distinct input configurations: AEF embeddings (AI-Ready data), traditional multi-source features (Analysis-Ready data), and the combination of both. The results demonstrate that: (1) The 64-dimensional AEF embeddings alone achieved an Overall Accuracy (OA) of 79%, significantly outperforming traditional multi-source features (OA = 73%). The combined inputs (combining AEF embeddings with traditional features) yielded performance identical to that of the AEF embeddings (OA = 79%, Kappa = 0.77), indicating that their combinations provided no discernible gain in accuracy. (2) AEF effectively mitigated spectral confusion among similar coniferous species (e.g., Spruce vs. Fir), increasing the F1-scores by approximately 10% in high-altitude zones compared to traditional methods; (3) The AEF-based workflow reduced total computational time by approximately 83% by obviating the need for complex preprocessing; (4) AEF embeddings showed improved label efficiency and better performance stability under topographic variability, achieving higher classification accuracy than traditional multi-source features under limited training samples (e.g., 20% of the dataset) and in steep mountainous terrain conditions; and (5) AEF embeddings showed good compatibility with ensemble-based non-linear classifiers such as RF, which achieved the strongest performance among the three classifiers. This study confirms that AEF can serve as a standalone, high-efficiency solution for regional-scale forest monitoring in complex mountainous terrain, demonstrating a clear representational advantage over traditional feature engineering within ensemble-based classification frameworks.

title: Edge-Oriented Bearing Fault Diagnosis via Triple-Lightweight Network with Adaptive Pruning
authors: Zhaokang Zhan, Siqi Zhang, Jiacan Xu, Dazhong Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Real-time diagnosis of motor bearing faults is essential for fault detection and timely maintenance, minimizing operational risks. However, most existing approaches often depend on cloud platforms or high-performance local servers, which introduce latency and potential security vulnerabilities due to raw data transmission. To overcome these limitations, this paper presents an adaptive pruning triple-lightweight network (APTL-net), which achieves model lightweighting for edge deployment across three stages: model construction, feature extraction, and structural pruning. First, a lightweight fault diagnosis framework optimized for edge environments is proposed, enabling parallel training and recursive inference to eliminate the traditional trade-off between training efficiency and inference performance. Second, a frequency-domain decomposition-based, weight-sharing multiscale convolution module is designed to efficiently extract multiscale features from periodic signals while harmonizing scale variations. Moreover, a fully automated adaptive pruning strategy is proposed to dynamically group and remove redundant structures during training without manual intervention. Finally, APTL-net is deployed on the edge device and validated on the physical platform equipped with real hardware. Experimental results indicate that APTL-net achieves a diagnostic accuracy of 99.54% while reducing parameters by 47.82%, FLOPs by 49.98%, and edge inference latency by 20.59%, thereby delivering superior performance over existing lightweight fault diagnosis networks.

title: Frequency Relocated PIDAFF Control Scheme for LFC in Microgrid Amid Multiple Cyber Threats
authors: Vivek Kumar, Yogesh V. Hote, G. Lloyds Raja, Suvra Pattanayak
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: The frequency regulations of advanced power gener-ation units are difficult to control due to constant load variations. The issues are further exacerbated by the system's vulnerability to cyber threats, which pose a serious threat and might destabilize the whole system. Traditional control methods frequently struggle to provide satisfactory performance and sufficient stability in these situations. To address these problems, this paper introduces a robust frequency-relocated proportional-integral-derivative-acceleration with fractional-order filter (PIDAFF) design based on the internal model control (IMC) strategy. The proposed control strategy uses a pole-zero adjustment parameter that indi-cates system resilience. Moreover, the resilience of the proposed control mechanism has also been tested using robust stability and fragility analysis. The performance & robustness of the proposed approach are investigated for the load frequency control problem of microgrids under cyber threats, model nonlinearity, and several load variations, such as step change, random change, and sinusoidal load changes. The proposed method demonstrates a significant enhancement over existing approaches based on integral errors and transient performance metrics. The proposed design reduces overshoot by 70.35%, 69.43% and 59.94%; un-dershoot by 90.3%, 90.7% and 73.49% than existing methods. Also, the settling time of the proposed design is reduced by 96.7% and 96.9% compared to well-known existing methods. Furthermore, the proposed concept is experimentally validated using the OPAL-RT (OP5600) platform to assess its practical applicability and effectiveness in real-world scenarios.

title: CDO-Net: A Distributional Optimization Network for Confidence Maps in Building Segmentation
authors: Wei Wang, Hao Cui, Ji Qi, Guo Zhang, Huabin Wang, Bingxuan Guo, Xinchang Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The geometric precision of building segmentation is frequently bottlenecked not by the feature extraction capabilities of models, but by the crude post-processing step of binarizing their uncertain confidence map outputs. This forced decision-making on ambiguous probabilities is the primary cause of boundary degradation and structural distortion. This paper introduces a paradigm shift, moving from direct result binarization to a learned optimization of the underlying confidence distribution itself. We propose the Confidence Distribution Optimization Network (CDO-Net), a novel post-processing framework designed to learn a transport-inspired mapping. It transforms the uncertain source distribution from any segmentation model into a sharp, geometrically-regular target distribution that mirrors an ideal building mask. To achieve this, CDO-Net integrates a dual-branch CNN-Transformer architecture, which captures both fine-grained local uncertainties and global structural context. This transformation is driven by a multi-faceted objective function that synergistically enforces pixel-level fidelity, distributional realism via adversarial learning, and structural coherence through geometric priors. As a universal plug-and-play module, CDO-Net provides a powerful solution for refining the outputs of diverse segmentation models. Extensive experiments on the Inria, Massachusetts, and WHU datasets verify the general effectiveness of the proposed module. When integrated with DeepLabv3, CDO-Net achieves a relative improvement of up to 5.67% in Boundary F1-score compared with the second-best model on the Inria dataset, while consistently enhancing overall metrics such as IoU, thereby substantiating the superiority of the proposed distribution optimization paradigm.
