title: Prescribed-time Adaptive Neural Control for Piezoelectric Micropositioning Stages with Event-triggered Communication
authors: Heyu Hu, Chengyuan Ma, Shengjun Wen, Changan Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: To ensure that the piezoelectric micropositioning stage achieves trajectory tracking within a prescribed time, this paper investigates an adaptive neural control method with prescribed-time convergence. First, a single-parameter neural network approximation technique is employed to compensate the hysteresis modelling error, effectively mitigating the influence of inherent hysteresis in the piezoelectric positioning platform and reducing computational complexity. Then, by introducing a performance function related to the convergence time and an error transformation function linked to the system error, the original prescribed-time tracking control problem is transformed into a general system for controller design. The notable advantage is that the convergence time of the original system can be arbitrarily specified through the introduced performance function, while the transformation function allows the controller to disregard initial errors. Furthermore, a prescribed-time adaptive neural control scheme is developed using a relative threshold-based triggering condition to reduce sampling errors caused by discontinuous transmission of control signals. Moreover, Lyapunov stability analysis is used to verify the designed controller’s effectiveness. Finally, the proposed method is experimentally validated on a piezoelectric positioning stage.

title: Whole-Body Coordination and Spinopelvic Alignment during Yoga Standing Maneuvers: Comparing Practitioners with Non-Practitioners_supp2-3697342.mp4
authors: Sunil Agrawal
publish: N/A

title: A Generalized Spatio-Temporal Causal Knowledge Graph Framework with Large Language Model-Enhanced Reasoning for Urban Event Management
authors: Gang Zheng
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Urban event management faces critical challenges in processing unstructured citizen complaints because of the limitations of traditional extraction models in handling colloquial text, static nature of conventional knowledge graphs that overlook spatiotemporal risk propagation, and brittleness of handcrafted symbolic rules that fail to generalize to novel scenarios. To address these issues, this study presents ST-CKG, a generalized spatiotemporal causal knowledge graph framework with Large Language Model (LLM)-enhanced reasoning. The framework integrates three tightly coupled components: a hybrid information extraction module that combines fine-tuned models with an LLM-based verifier to produce confidence-calibrated structured knowledge; a spatiotemporal causal hypergraph construction module that encodes spatial proximity and temporal causal hyperedges to model cascading urban risks; and a neuro-symbolic reasoning engine that distills expert-defined risk causal chain rules into a graph attention network, enabling interpretable and generalizable inference over incomplete or unseen event configurations. Extensive experiments conducted on over 2,000 real-world citizen complaints demonstrated that the hybrid extraction module outperformed standalone baselines by 3–5 percentage points in the F1-score, achieving 91% urgency assessment accuracy, significantly surpassing pure rule-based and pure graph neural network variants in rule-uncovered scenarios. Ablation studies have confirmed the indispensability of each component. The ST-CKG framework offers a replicable paradigm for bridging unstructured civic narratives with structured actionable governance decisions in smart cities.

title: Scalable Hybrid LSH-Based Framework for Structural Code Clone Detection
authors: Dasun Wickramasooriya, Bilal Awshid, Hasintha Dilshan, Sudila Jayasekara, Nuwan Kodagoda, Kalpani Manathunga
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The identification of code clones is a fundamental challenge in large-scale software maintenance and academic integrity. While Type-1 and Type-2 clones can be effectively identified through syntactic matching, Type-3 clones, characterized by structural reorganizations, require deeper semantic understanding that exceeds simple token comparison. This paper addresses the research question of how to design a detection framework that accurately identifies Type-1, Type-2, and Type-3 clones while maintaining sub-quadratic scalability suitable for massive repositories. To resolve this, a hybrid multi-stage framework is proposed that integrates deterministic normalization for exact matches with a supervised machine learning pipeline for complex variants. Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves an aggregate F1-Score of 95.53% and a high recall of 96.13 %. By applying Locality-Sensitive Hashing, the system achieved a 51% reduction in the pairwise search space and a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.6 \times$</tex> speedup in total processing time. In addition, Recursive Feature Elimination determined an optimal feature set that comprised three significant features: the Token Set Dice Coefficient (key factor), AST Node Count (structural indicator), and Cyclomatic Complexity (complexity indicator), all of which contributed significantly and independently to the accuracy of detection. The resulting framework provides a scalable and effective solution for conducting automated software audits, ensuring high detection coverage while significantly reducing computational overhead.

title: A Centralized Heuristic for Energy Balancing in Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Hüseyin Emre Kankaya
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Energy imbalance is a primary factor limiting the lifetime of wireless sensor networks (WSNs). This paper proposes a centralized and routing-agnostic energy balancing algorithm that computes node-specific probabilities for alternating between direct transmission to the sink and hop-by-hop forwarding. The probabilities are determined at the sink through a virtual simulation of network-wide data flows and energy consumption, without affecting the actual network state. Simulation results under diverse deployment scenarios including irregular areas and non-uniform node distributions demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves energy balance, extends network lifetime, and increases data delivery compared to pure direct and pure hop-by-hop communication schemes.

title: HADASI: The Human-ADAS Interaction Standard for Sustainable Driving Behaviors
authors: Minh Sang Pham Do, Anh Tuan Vu, Gerrit Meixner
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: In the 67-year history of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) development, many researchers and companies have tried to improve the technical functions to recognize hazardous incidents on the road, so-called External Scenarios (ES). Later, during the digital transformation era, scholars used modern technologies to study ADAS activation scenarios in which drivers are fatigued or distracted (Internal Scenarios-IS). Nevertheless, drivers in countries with heavy motorbike traffic, such as Vietnam and China, complain that ADAS notifications become annoying because many motorbikes frequently cut in front of their cars. Consequently, they choose not to use ADAS at all. Hence, we found a research gap that previous studies have neglected: the improvement of human-machine interaction by deactivating ADAS when drivers are already focused on the road. This gap needs to be researched carefully; otherwise, it will put drivers in danger. Therefore, we propose the Human-ADAS Interaction standard (HADASI) by leveraging our previous work on the Traffic Generator for Driving Simulators, along with dashcam tracking and driver face recording. Besides, from legal theories in Criminology and Victimology, we can see that the traffic context is a novel third factor that affects the relationship between the known factors (driver behaviors and road incidents). The positions and angle differences between drivers' vision and their vehicle direction are used to help scholars understand these traffic contexts. Thus, we utilize our existing Action Point Angle of Sight (APAS) formula to prove that the theory is possible to implement in a realistic traffic simulation.

title: Early Identification of Alzheimer’s Disease using Blood Plasma Protein Profiles: A Comparative Study of XGBoost and SVM Algorithms
authors: P. Anitha, V. Arul Gnanam, N.Prakash, E. Loganathan, E. Nithyakala, S.Nivetha
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: the purpose of this research will lie in improving the accuracy of predictability while examining the results of the XGBoost process with regard to performance compared to the results of the Support Vector Machine technique. Two independent groups of models were created for the evaluation task, with Group 1 including the XGBoost model, a learner of complicated patterns from high-dimensional plasma protein datasets via the concept of gradient boosting, and Group 2 including the SVM classifier model, an optimally dividing hyperplane-based classification learner. The experiments were performed 20 times for comparison. The accuracy value of XGBoost is 90.60%, and the accuracy value of SVM is 85.10%. In addition, the result indicates that the performance of the XGBoost model is more stable with low variance. This can be seen from the lower value of the standard deviation and the standard error compared to the value for the SVM model. The result is statistically meaningful since the probability value is below 0.05. Therefore, on accuracy and consistency and Overall precision, the results for the XGBoost algorithm were higher than those for the SVM classifier, featuring their usefulness in Alzheimer's prediction based on plasma from blood. The XGBoost-based models can be usedn medical decision support systems.

title: Benchmarking Transformer and xLSTM for Time-Series Forecasting of Heat Consumption
authors: Marja Wahl, Daniel R. Bayer, Sven Rausch, Marco Pruckner
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Obtaining an accurate short-term forecasting for heat demand is an essential part of operating district heating networks cost-efficient and reliable. Heat consumption time series at the building level are highly dependent on exogenous variables such as outdoor temperature and individual usage patterns, making forecasting in this context a challenging task. Thus, this paper benchmarks novel Transformer-based and xLSTM architectures for short-term heat-demand forecasting. Using hourly data from 25 German buildings (2017—2025), we compare three-hour and 24-hour forecasting horizons relevant for intraday control and day-ahead scheduling. We establish a multi-building benchmark that tests whether models trained on pooled, heterogeneous building data are able to generalize across diverse building stock. The results show that the xLSTM achieves the lowest RMSE (19.88 kWh for three-hour, 21.47 kWh for 24-hour forecasts), while the Temporal Fusion Transformer attains the best MAE (9.16 kWh for three-hour forecasts). As xLSTMs and Transformers require long training times and have a huge number of trainable parameters, their sustainability remains questionable. Therefore, this paper further investigates the trade-off between predictive accuracy and computational resource demand of the evaluated forecasting models. The findings indicate that also low-parameter models like a traditional fullyconnected network achieve good predictive results, highlighting that marginal accuracy gains of the novel prediction models come at substantial resource expense for this use case.

title: Smart Redundant Management of a Next-Generation Critical Power Grid Infrastructure
authors: Srdjan Skok, Dunja Srpak, Dubravko Sabolic, Sasa Aksentijevic
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Next-generation power grids with high penetration of distributed and converter-based resources require communication infrastructures that ensure deterministic, secure, and resilient operation. Conventional wired communication systems represent vulnerable single points of failure, particularly under physical and cyber-physical threats such as targeted drone attacks on critical grid assets. This paper introduces a smart redundant management concept that employs fifth-generation mobile communication technology to support monitoring, protection, and control functions of critical power grid infrastructure. By exploiting low-latency and high-reliability features of 5G networks, the proposed approach enhances operational resilience and continuity of control under degraded and disrupted communication conditions.

title: Machine Learning-Based Optimization of Product Loading in Airline In-Flight Sales
authors: Serkan Kocoglu, Zuleyha Akusta Dagdeviren
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Airline in-flight retail operations face a critical inventory challenge: determining the optimal quantity of perishable products to load onto each flight. Overstocking leads to spoilage, disposal, and unnecessary fuel costs from transporting excess weight, while understocking results in lost revenue and diminished passenger satisfaction. Traditional forecasting models are typically trained to minimize symmetric error metrics, which fail to reflect the asymmetric economic consequences of overstocking and understocking. This study proposes a cost-aware machine learning framework that reformulates this inventory challenge as an asymmetric optimization task grounded in the classical Newsvendor model. A custom loss function is introduced to explicitly model the economic penalties associated with these decisions. The loss function is integrated into a multi-strategy ensemble framework combining Random Forest, LightGBM, CatBoost, and a Zero-Inflated LightGBM model. The ensemble employs four optimization strategies: multi-start global weight optimization via Differential Evolution, Newsvendoraligned quantile-shrinkage calibration, per-level joint weight-bias optimization across minimum-stock constraints, and cost-guided integer rounding. The framework is evaluated on 362,732 empirical flight records drawn from a commercial airline. Results show that the proposed approach reduces total operational cost to $679,680, corresponding to a 55.4 % reduction compared to the human-decision baseline of $1,525,355, while strictly adhering to minimum service constraints. The findings demonstrate that aligning machine learning objectives with operational cost structures can substantially improve decision quality in revenue-critical retail contexts with asymmetric cost structures.

title: Optimisation and Performance Evaluation of Hilbert Curve Structured Antenna for Wireless Application
authors: Mary Joy Kinol, Marshiana.D, Krishnamoorthy. N.R
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: The outcomes have been carefully studied, and the design has been tweaked for better performance to meet today’s wireless needs. It sharpens a number of key electromagnetic traits: return loss, gain, radiation patterns, and overall power efficiency. More precisely, it yields a return loss of –31.496 dB at 6.1 GHz and –24.8063 dB at 6.9 GHz, which shows that it is strongly impedance-matched. A gain of 3.2 dB points to more focused radiation, whereas the accepted power figures-26.5 dB at 6.1 GHz and 28.5 dB at 6.9 GHz-predict efficient energy usage. Radiated power stands at 23.5 dB and 24 dB, with the associated radiation efficiencies of 0.16 dB and 0.70 dB, respectively, underlining improved transmission capability. All put together, these results suggest that the optimised Hilbert Curve Structured Antenna is well-suited for applications in satellite systems, radar, and advanced wireless systems, and it provides a sound basis for further work to be directed toward compactness, bandwidth, and operational efficiency.

title: A Modern Quadratic Trapezoidal Fuzzy Framework for Meticulous Blood Cell Sectionalization and Discernment
authors: Catherina A J, Jagadeeswari Murugan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: Accurate blood cell segmentation is essential for biomedical image analysis but remains challenging due to noise, staining variability, intensity inhomogeneity, and ambiguous cell boundaries. Recent techniques, including thresholding, clustering, edge-based methods, fuzzy logic models, and deep learning approaches, have been widely investigated. However, classical methods are often sensitive to noise and low contrast, while conventional fuzzy models rely on linear membership functions that produce abrupt transitions and imprecise boundaries. Deep learning methods, although effective, require large annotated datasets and high computational resources. To address these challenges, this work proposes a novel quadratic trapezoidal fuzzy number (QTZFN) and QTZFN-based segmentation framework to achieve smooth nonlinear uncertainty modeling at the pixel level. The objective is to improve segmentation robustness and boundary representation without relying on learning-based models. Experimental results on a publicly available blood cell segmentation dataset demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms classical and fuzzy-based baselines, with statistical significance confirming its effectiveness.

title: Evaluation of Multi-Modal Object Detection and Fusion Strategies for Automatic Train Operation
authors: Fırat Bozkaya, Tankut Acarman
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Multimodal object detection improves the performance of Automatic Train Operation. Perception of signals, tracks, transitions, and dynamic objects such as pedestrians and trains over long distances is necessary for advanced railway automation, particularly Grade of Automation 3 (GoA3) and Grade of Automation 4 (GoA4), corresponding to driverless and unattended train operation, respectively. In this study, three sensing modalities formed by the suite of a RGB, an infrared (IR), and a radar sensor, two fusion schemes such as early fusion using 4-channel Radar and RGB encoding, and late fusion through Weighted Boxes Fusion (WBF) are evaluated and tested. YOLOv8 and YOLOv11 are used as one-stage detector to analyze the impact of dense convolutions versus attention-based feature extraction in the deep learning detector architecture. The OSDaR23 dataset is used. The results illustrate that early fusion systematically improves over single-modality baselines and YOLOv11 outperforms YOLOv8. Multimodality improves detection of small and distant objects in the railway scene under low-visibility conditions, and radar provides complementary structural cues. Mean average precision (mAP) metric is improved by early fusion and multimodality of RGB and 4-channel radar using YOLOv11 by 2.1% versus the RGB single modality. By fusing radar and RGB sensor, the multimodality improves the mAP by 2.45% versus the RGB sensor stand-alone modality and 125.14% for the radar modality using the day dataset, and by 1.79% for RGB and 376.77% for radar using the night dataset. This study contributes to ATO object detection research by evaluating and testing multimodality with early and late fusion, and two one-stage detectors with different modules in their architectures.

title: Explainable Prediction of Daily Stress and Sleep Quality from Passive Smartphone Behavioral Sensing: A SHAP-Based Analysis of the StudentLife Dataset
authors: Jibrailu Daniel, Chahyaandida Ishaya, Ndachimya Magaji Edward, Vimipwa Augustine, Yusuf Garga Bello, Louis Adamu Obadiah, Suleiman Wilson, Zidon Musa, Douglas Kilyobas Titus
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Chronic stress and poor sleep quality are among the most prevalent mental health concerns in university student populations. While passive smartphone sensing offers a non-intrusive alternative to traditional clinical assessments, it remains unclear which behavioral signals are most predictive of daily stress and sleep outcomes, and whether complex machine learning models provide meaningful advantages over simple baselines in this domain. This study addresses both questions using the StudentLife dataset—a longitudinal passive sensing dataset collected from 48 Dartmouth College undergraduates over a 10-week academic term. Seven daily behavioral features are extracted from activity sensing, conversation sensing, and phone usage logs. Five regression models—Linear Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)—are evaluated under Leave-One-Subject-Out (LOSO) cross-validation, the standard protocol for subject-independent generalization in mobile health research. Results show that Linear Regression achieves competitive performance for both tasks (stress: MAE=1.242, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>=0.008; sleep: MAE=1.416, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>=0.112), while complex ensemble and deep learning models provide no consistent advantage. SHAP explainability analysis reveals that physical activity ratio and conversation duration are the strongest behavioral predictors of daily stress, while week in term and conversation frequency dominate sleep prediction. We position the near-zero R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> values for stress prediction not as a failure but as a rigorous quantification of how much variance in daily stress is explainable by population-level behavioral features alone under subject-independent evaluation—a negative result of methodological value to the mobile health community. These findings contribute a rigorous methodological benchmark and motivate future work on personalized, multimodal sensing frameworks.

title: Decision-Centric, Regime-Aware Adaptive Ensemble for Financial Market Movement Prediction
authors: K Srinivasulu Achari, Jakkam Harika Reddy, Gumpu Giri Prasanna, Arshid Vaishnavi, Gattu Marieta Angel, K.Nagaraju
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Financial market movement prediction is a longstanding problem since financial time series are non-stationary, assume noise, and are volatile in nature. The traditional forecasting models that focus on price accuracy in a numerical sense will be poorly correlated with the demands and information requirements of real trading and risk management where orientation accuracy and capability to withstand market conditions are the more important factors. This study suggests a regime-wise, decision-oriented adaptive ensemble model of predicting financial market moves. Instead of modelling price level, the method develops forecasting as a directional classification problem and dynamically balances the contributions of the ensemble models based on the current market regimes. The framework combines volatility-based regime completion with adaptive ensemble fusion scheme that uses heterogeneous base learners which learns regime specific adaptive weights. The design permits the ensemble to remix predictive power amid both linear and nonlinear designs in a low and high volatility network, resulting in enhanced captivity to varying market convictions. An experiment is performed based on a publicly available S&P proxy dataset based on an index of the 500 biggest companies in the stock market to test the hypothesis on a walk-forward validation scheme to capture realistic predictive conditions. It is found that the adaptive ensemble has balanced performance, with an overall F1-score of 0.628, as opposed to 0.710 with Logistic Regression, 0.602 with Random Forest and 0.640 with a fixed ensemble. Evaluation regime-wise shows furthermore better performance in times of high volatility, with the adaptive ensemble having a F1-score of 0.641 compared to 0.610 in times of low volatility. The results indicate predictability and consistency of directional forecasts.

title: Experimental Validation of a Modular Low-Voltage Battery Management System With Model-Based Architecture
authors: Yiğit Burak Varol, Fikret Çalışkan
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The design and experimental validation of a modular low-voltage battery management system (LV-BMS) for low-voltage electric vehicle subsystems and second-life battery applications are presented in this study. A key contribution of the study is the embedded integration and experimental validation of functions that are typically addressed separately in the literature, including OCV-based initialization, Coulomb counting, a 3RC equivalent circuit model with an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), coulomb-throughput-based State of Health (SoH) estimation, and passive single-cell balancing, within a unified architecture using repurposed cells. While the model-based voltage feedback layer reduces long-term drift under dynamic loading conditions, the current-integration layer provides fast short-term response. A four-cell battery pack was used for experimental validation under controlled operating conditions. At the end of the discharge test, the Coulomb Counting method produced a 39.5 % relative SoC estimation error with respect to the reference value, whereas the EKF-based method reduced this error to approximately 3.8 %. The results demonstrate that the proposed architecture provides an embedded-feasible and experimentally validated LVBMS solution for safe and reliable battery operation.

title: LankaLens: A Mobile-Based Heritage Exploration System for Artifact Reconstruction and Location Tracking
authors: Herath H M H S, Dissanayaka D U C, Gunawardhana D D M S P, Bandara M M K G, Aruna Ishara Gamage, Nushkan Nismi
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Digital technologies are increasingly used to enhance visitor engagement and interpretation at archaeological heritage sites. However, many locations still lack integrated mobile systems that combine immersive visualization with structured on-site exploration. The main objective of this research is to develop a mobile-based heritage exploration system that integrates mixed reality reconstruction with real-time location-aware navigation. This study presents LankaLens, an Android-based application that supports two key components: mixed reality artifact visualization using QR-triggered reconstruction and a real-time user location tracking and exploration map system. The system integrates geolocation services, QR-based verification, and cloudsupported exploration tracking to improve visitor interaction with heritage environments. Sigiriya, Sri Lanka, is used as the primary implementation environment. The results demonstrate that the system achieved GPS accuracy between 6.5 m and 8.6 m with geofence detection delays ranging from 1.3 s to 2.1 s, while maintaining 100 % successful QR-based visit verification. These findings confirm the effectiveness of integrating mixed reality visualization with real-time location-aware navigation in heritage environments.

title: Extractive Text Summarization using biased, adaptive and topic-aware Katz Centrality
authors: Sonali Mhatre, Lata Ragha
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Text summarization gives a condensed and more concise version of a given large text. Automatic Text Summarization can be achieved using either an abstractive or extractive approach. Abstractive methods primarily use deep learning models to generate a concise version of the text. Extractive methods are classified into supervised and unsupervised. Supervised methods rely on labeled datasets to determine which sentences from the original text should be included in the summary. Unsupervised methods often use graph-based approaches to generate summaries. To ensure better coverage and minimize redundancy, this work contributes by modifying Katz centrality considering sentence position bias, semantic bias and text length aware long path influence. Each sentence is represented as a topic-distribution vector and modeled as a node in a similarity graph, where edges capture topic-level proximity between sentence pairs. The graph is then processed using an updated Katz centrality algorithm to rank sentences for selection in the summary. The resulting summaries are subsequently compared with those generated using the TextRank algorithm.

title: A Real World Deployable Deep Learning System for Early Skin Cancer Screening from Skin Images
authors: Mahmut Furkan Bakal, Aysu İrem Adem, Arda Sezen, Naz Dumanoğlu
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Skin cancer is a very common and dangerous type of cancer worldwide. Early diagnosis and timely treatment are critically important for patients' survival and treatment success. However, current skin cancer diagnostic systems require long waiting times for appointments and examinations at hospitals. To alleviate this problem, our team has developed a skin cancer diagnostic application using artificial intelligence and deep learning techniques. Designed as a mobile application, our system is built on a YOLO11-CLS-based classification model and considers the ABCD rule (Asymmetry, Border Irregularity, Color, Diameter), commonly used in dermatology, as a supporting decision mechanism. Due to the large number of images and class variations, the ISIC2019 dataset was used in our study. In the tests conducted, our model achieved an overall accuracy rate of 89.23%. The application allows users to automatically classify lesions by uploading their own skin photos by using their phones. Furthermore, it improves analysis quality by applying pre-processing steps such as hair removal through the image processing methods used. Furthermore, the application's history page allows for tracking changes in lesions by storing images of the same mole taken at different times, supporting early detection. Through early detection and advice, the system seeks to support patients' treatment procedures. Along with its health benefits, it may also help people get access to healthcare, increase awareness, and motivate them to frequently check the condition of their own skin. In these ways, the created application is a major advancement in technology solutions for the identification of skin cancer and seeks to have a beneficial and significant influence on public health.

title: Junctionless Shallow Extension Engineered Ferroelectric (FET) for Threshold Voltage Reduction and Short Channel Effect Suppression
authors: Sandhya Tiwari, Anurag Rungta, Shalu Grag, Neeraj
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive simulation-based investigation of a Junctionless Shallow Extension Engineered Ferroelectric Field-Effect Transistor (JL-SEE-Fe FET). In the proposed device, a ferroelectric layer of Si:HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> is incorporated as the gate stack, while HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> and SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> are employed as dielectric layer. A single dielectric pocket is engineered on the source side, which suppresses short-channel effects (SCEs) and reduces leakage current by mitigating the electric field. Key device characteristics, including surface potential, drain current (Ids), transconductance (gm), output conductance (gd), and total gate capacitance (CGG), are extracted and analyzed. In addition, high-frequency performance metrics such as maximum transfer power gain (MTPG) and unilateral power gain (UPG) are evaluated to assess the suitability of the device for RF and analog circuit applications. For validation, the performance of the JL-SEE-Fe FET is compared with that of a conventional junctionless FET (JL-FET). The simulations are performed using the ATLAS-3D device simulator.

title: Lightweight Blockchain-Inspired Intrusion Detection Framework for CPS
authors: Abhishek Bajpai, Vineet Kansal, Abhishek Singh, Shiv Prakash
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) are becoming victims of advanced cyberattacks, which require robust and un-hackable intrusion detection systems. This work provides a lightweight blockchain-inspired intrusion detection system which is a hybrid of real-time monitoring and log immutability. The suggested system logs system events, such as file access, USB additions, process actions, and network access, and saves them through a dual-storage system, consisting of SQLite and local blockchain inspired immutable ledger. Such design assures high integrity audit trails, speedy log validation, and anti-tamper design. Experimental analysis in controlled CPS-like environments shows detection rates of up to 98 percent, with low false positives and blockchain insertion latency that can be used in real-time operations of CPS. The suggested framework has contributed to a higher level of forensic reliability as well as resistance to log manipulation which was highly jeopardized in traditional IDS and the resource overhead is minimal. The findings suggest that the lightweight blockchain-based IDS can serve as a helpful security protection in resource-constrained CPS environments.

title: Evaluating the Effect of Agile Maturity Levels on Long-Term Software Project Adaptability in Multinational Enterprises
authors: Velmurugan S, Anusya S, Sundaram K Meenakshi
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: The use of Agile methodologies in multinational companies is seen to enhance flexibility and responsiveness to software development project, but the long run adaptability of software projects highly depends on the maturity of Agile in organizations. Against the background of sustainable digital change and a robust software infrastructure, the research assesses how the Agile levels of maturity influence long-term software project adaptation within multinational enterprise settings. The study deploys an Agile maturity assessment framework by analysing how differences in institutionalization of processes, sustained learning, stakeholder collaboration, and cross-team coordination have an effect in the adaptability in long-term project lifecycles. Significant adaptability indicators are the resilience to change, innovativeness potential, sustainable workforce knowledge and the permanence of delivery performances. The comparative analysis between low, medium, and high levels of Agile maturity shows that the more mature a company is, the more sustainable its adaptability and its readiness to innovate over the long term are crucial, whereas the less mature is the company, the less adaptable it is to operational changes in the short-term. The results indicate that Agile maturity is an important facilitator of responsible software engineering practices, which enable resilient digital infrastructure and long-term organizational growth.

title: FPGA Implementation of High-Speed Nikhilam Sutra based Vedic Multiplier for Cryptographic Applications
authors: Ramya R, Akash S, Rajmohan M G
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: In modern digital systems, multiplication is a fundamental arithmetic operation widely used in applications such as signal processing, cryptography, and computer communications. This paper presents the design and implementation of a high-speed Vedic multiplier based on Nikhilam Sutra. The proposed approach provides an efficient multiplication technique that reduces computational complexity compared to conventional binary multiplication algorithms. The Vedic multiplier based on Nikhilam Sutra, is simulated using ModelSim software and implemented on Cyclone II FPGA board with Altera Quartus software. To evaluate the real-time performance of the proposed architecture, its functionality and performance are compared with a conventional binary multiplier. Performance analysis is carried out for various bit-widths to highlight the advantages of the Vedic methodology. For example, the 16-bit Vedic multiplier utilizes only 68 logic elements, whereas the binary multiplier requires 255 logic elements, which is approximately 3.75× higher than the proposed design. The propagation delay is reduced from 39.385 ns to 22.325 ns, resulting in approximately 43% speed improvement over the conventional binary multiplier. To demonstrate its practical applicability, the Vedic multiplier was integrated into the RSA cryptosystem, a widely used public-key encryption algorithm in modern cryptography. Both the encryption and decryption processes of RSA were implemented using Vedic multiplier. The entire system was simulated using ModelSim, and the results were verified for correctness, confirming the effectiveness of the design in cryptographic applications.

title: Tools of the Modern Historian
authors: David G. Michelson
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: Astronaut Gus Grissom once defended the effort devoted to the U.S. manned lunar landing program, which at its peak employed over 400,000 workers across the nation and comprised over 4% of the U.S. federal budget, by noting that, “In the final analysis, only man can fully evaluate the Moon in terms understandable to other men” [1]. It is telling that Grissom felt that the best justification for Apollo was not the new technical capabilities being developed nor the spinoffs that would result, but how Apollo would contribute to the human experience.

title: A Real-Time Multi-Sensor Fusion Framework for UAV Threat Assessment
authors: Petek Şener, Gül Erten, Ahmet Gökhan Göze, Melih Can Hamurcu, Çağlar Akman, Mehmet Kılıçarslan
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Real-time assessment of environmental threats is a critical requirement for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) operating in dynamic indoor environments to achieve safe navigation. In this study, a system is proposed that represents objects in the environment with specific features and computes real-time threat levels based on position and motion information. The system, which integrates data from multiple sensors, represents each object with a token structure encompassing distance, relative motion, orientation, and confidence parameters. To ensure computational efficiency and stability, a fixed-size state vector is constructed using a top-K approach that prioritizes the most critical objects. The framework produces continuous threat scores for each object, providing decision support to higher-level navigation components. Implemented on a ROS2based architecture, the system has been extensively tested in indoor environment simulations containing dynamic obstacles. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach can deliver stable and reliable threat assessment under varying scenario conditions.

title: Centralized Home and Workspace Safety Integration Box using ESP-32
authors: Balasubramanian V, Aadhi Vigneshwar P S, Adithyan S
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: It is essential to ensure security and real-time observation in both residential and business premises but the traditional type of systems frequently work independently and thus leads to disjointed control and sluggish reactions. This project suggests a Centralized Safety and Monitoring System combining the use of a number of sensors and the ESP32 NodeMCU through which the central control unit will run. The major sensors are Gas, PIR, DHT11, and ZMPT101B, SCT013 and ESP32 Camera that have enabled full access to environmental, electrical and security data. The sensor data is sent to an IoT cloud platform using Wi-Fi to allow offsite monitoring using the Blynk mobile app. The system has realistic safety measures that include buzzers, LEDs, appliancecontrol that is powered with a relay, and manual override buttons. The centralization of monitoring and automation of responses are beneficial features of the system as they increase efficiency of operations, minimal manual interventions and promptness of hazards and intrusions alerts. The combination of these two solutions creates a powerful and scalable solution to the contemporary smart environment.

title: HfO2 Nanoparticles on SiOx Nanowires: Nanoplasmonics Insights into Helicobacter Pylori
authors: Shubhro Chakrabartty, Kashyap Ramteke, Debjani Dutta, Kumud Kumar Das
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: H. Pylori (Helicobacter pylori) is a unique, distinctive bacterium (spiral-shaped) that thrives in a low-oxygen environment. It typically measures <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.5-1 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> wide and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2-4 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> in length, often characterized by a short spiral or S-shape. This microorganism is uniquely adapted to colonize the human stomach, primarily residing in the pyloric region, a remarkable feat given the organ's intensely acidic environment. According to recent findings, there is an undeniable correlation between being infected with H. Pylori and developing peptic ulcers, gastric mucosal inflammation, and gastric cancer. Timely detection of H. Pylori is therefore critical to mitigate its adverse effects. This paper investigates the annealed HfO2 Nanoparticles (NPs)/SiOx Nanowires (NWs) nanoplasmonics biosensors, emphasizing the pivotal role of Hafnium dioxide (HfO2), which is a highly promising material for plasmonic applications due to its superior optical characteristics. Such enabling factors include high dielectric constant (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{k}=16-25$</tex>), wide band gap (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5.6-6 \text{eV}$</tex>), broad band optical transparency (UV to mid-IR), and excellent chemical stability, which are discussed in depth. Quantitative optical characterization revealed a high sensitivity of 800 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{nm} /$</tex> RIU, a quality factor (QF) as high as 17, and an exceptional figure of merit (FoM) of 20 RIU-1, confirming strong plasmonic confinement and efficient biomolecular interaction transduction. The biosensing device achieved 3.75 x 10-4 RIU detection limit, indicating its capability to detect extremely small refractive index variations associated with H. pylori-induced biochemical changes. Furthermore, a relative detection accuracy of 20% validated the resolvability of the induced spectral shift with respect to the resonance linewidth.

title:
Quantum-Enhanced Real-Time CO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Classification System for Urban Environmental Monitoring
authors: German Granados, Carlos Sierra Arreaga, Maria Mora Quinde, Nathaly Burgos Tomala, Washington Velasquez
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Urban environmental management in developing cities, faces the significant challenge of monitoring and responding to carbon dioxide (CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) emissions in real-time. This paper presents an innovative system that integrates simulated quantum algorithms with modern data processing technologies for the dynamic classification of CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> levels in urban environments. The methodology employs an interactive web interface that allows users to select geographic locations. It obtains real-time meteorological data, including temperature, humidity, and wind speed, through an API and processes this information using a quantum circuit. The system not only provides immediate CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> estimations and classifications but also features unique history tracking and CSV export functionality for retrospective analysis. Experiments conducted demonstrated an overall accuracy of 100% in classifying CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> levels. This approach shows the feasibility of applying quantum computing to create practical tools that support decision-making in the management of smart and resilient cities in the face of climate change.

title: InsightPlug: Addressing Consumer Cost Misperceptions for Electric Vehicles Through Local Scenarios and LLM-Driven Explanations
authors: Yulin Zeng, Sharon Hsiao, Yuhong Liu
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Electric vehicle (EV) adoption is often hindered by consumer misperceptions about affordability and long-term savings. These misperceptions arise not from insufficient information, but from the complexity of evaluating economic trade-offs that depend on local driving patterns, energy prices, and vehicle characteristics. This paper presents InsightPlug, a decision-support system designed to reduce consumer cost misperceptions by supporting economic reasoning rather than exhaustive calculation. Grounded in Becker's household production theory, the system frames EV adoption as a trade-off governed by money and time constraints and surfaces a small set of localized cost signals: Monthly Surplus, Daily Asset Utilization, and Charging Interval. These signals are augmented with per-user, natural-language explanations generated by a large language model, translating abstract metrics into interpretable economic narratives that support belief updating and improve understanding of EV cost trade-offs.

title: Quantitative Analysis of Coherence Length and Phase Noise Effects on Interferometric Fiber Optic Sensors
authors: Ezgi Yağmur Çakmak Cebesoy, Arif Dolma
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Interferometric fiber optic sensors offer high accuracy due to their phase difference-based measurement principles and are widely used in many industrial applications. However, the performance of these sensors is directly affected by fundamental physical parameters such as phase noise and the coherence length of the optical source. In this study, the effects of phase noise and coherence length on interferometric sensor performance were investigated using analytical expressions. In this study, the effect of phase noise on the minimum detectable length variation and the role of coherence length on interference visibility were analyzed separately. However, to evaluate the combined effect of these two parameters on system behavior, a multiparameter analysis approach was developed, and system performance was examined using an effective visibility-based model. In this context, the system behavior for different coherence lengths and the effect of phase noise on the minimum detectable length were numerically analyzed and graphically evaluated in the MATLAB. Furthermore, Mach-Zehnder, Michelson, Fabry–Perot, and Sagnac interferometric structures were evaluated comparatively, and performance differences under different operating conditions were revealed. The results show that a high coherence length alone is not sufficient, and that phase noise is also a critical parameter determining sensor performance. This study presents an analytical framework that considers both phase noise and coherence length for evaluating the performance of interferometric fiber optic sensors and provides an application-oriented assessment in terms of sensor design and optical source selection.

title: Machine Learning based Event Detection using Crowd-Sensed Data
authors: Arulprakash M, Sai Naga Surya Boyina, Aman Roy Valiveti
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: The proliferation of mobile devices and Internet-of-Things (IoT) sensors has made it possible to conduct massive crowdsourcing of data to deliver high-quality and timely information regarding people and the environmental state. Applications that can be supported using this data to detect events automatically include emergency response, urban surveillance and citizen safety. In this work, there is a machine learning-based framework of identifying events based on crowd-sensed data, which employs a leakage-sensitive modeling pipeline and real-time simulation environment. The system suggested conducts an exploratory data analysis, feature engineering, and group-based train-test splitting in order to avoid data leakage at the user level. Several models - Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Logistic Regression, and K-Means clustering are trained and compared. In order to improve interpretability, feature importance based on permutation is used, and distributional changes in streaming data are monitored by statistical drift detection through Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Live simulation module provides alerts depending on thresholds that are set up, which illustrates the applicability of the system to real-time. It is demonstrated that the ensemble techniques produce strong performance in the case of heterogeneous user and location patterns, which indicates that the crowd-sensed features represent a good choice when it comes to predicting events reliably. The framework combines the management of model registries and reproducible evaluation, which offers a scalable and explainable method of intelligent event monitoring in dynamic settings.

title: Hybrid ECG Arrhythmia Classification Using Fuzzy C-Means Enhanced Feature Fusion and Machine Learning
authors: Sema Yıldırım, İbrahim Arslanoğlu, Cem Haydaroğlu
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) signal classification plays a critical role in the early and reliable detection of cardiovascular diseases. However, noise contamination, high variability, and inter-class overlap significantly limit the performance of conventional methods, particularly in multi-class arrhythmia detection. To address these challenges, this study proposes a hybrid machine learning (ML) framework integrating Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering for enhanced feature representation. The experiments are conducted on the PhysioNet MIT-BIH Arrhythmia dataset, consisting of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 1 0, 0 0 0}$</tex> samples, divided into <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 0 \%}$</tex> training and 20% testing sets. ECG signals are preprocessed using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.5-40 \text{Hz}$</tex> band-pass filter, and heartbeat segments are extracted via R-peak detection. Each sample is represented by time-series data points, from which statistical and structural features (mean, standard deviation, maximum, minimum, range, and energy) are derived. The obtained fuzzy membership values are fused with the original features to construct a hybrid feature space. Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and XGBoost classifiers are then trained using both classical and FCM-enhanced feature sets. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed FCM-based feature fusion improves classification performance, particularly for ensemble learning models. The RF model achieves the highest performance (accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.9999$</tex>), while XGBoost shows notable improvement compared to the classical feature-based approach (accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 0.9996$</tex>). The SVM model maintains stable and consistent performance across both scenarios (accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx \mathbf{0. 9 9 6}$</tex>). Overall, the findings indicate that incorporating fuzzy membership information enhances feature representation by effectively modeling uncertainty and class overlap, leading to more robust and reliable multi-class ECG classification.

title: An AI-Driven Multi-Objective Optimization Model for Sensor-Based LED Lighting and Urban Place-Making
authors: Danying Chen, Zuhao Liu
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: With the development of smart cities, urban public space lighting is evolving from a simple form of illumination to a medium that carries culture and emotions. However, the existing intelligent lighting systems overly focus on energy efficiency, while seriously neglecting “local creation” and “human emotional experience”. To address this, this study proposes and validates an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven multi-objective optimization model for LED lighting based on IoT multimodal sensors. The research draws theoretical inspiration from contemporary public art (such as the light and shadow installations by Tatsuo Miyajima and Toyomi Hoshina), extracting three core parameters: “individual heterogeneity rhythm”, “biological electric drive randomness”, and “memory narrative space anchor”. Based on this, this paper constructs a real-world experimental framework integrating passive infrared, environmental lightsensitive, and plant bioelectric potential sensors, and uses the non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm (NSGA-II) as the core AI computational approach to collaboratively optimize three constraints: lighting energy consumption, visual/emotional comfort, and digital place creation index. The field deployment results indicate that this AI-driven dynamic lighting model significantly mitigates the “light island effect” of traditional sensor-based lighting, achieving a 42.5% system-level energy saving. At the same time, by introducing ecological micro-electropotential and personalized light and shadow interaction, the observed average stay time of pedestrians in the urban decline square is extended by nearly three times (from 3.5 to 12.4 minutes), significantly enhancing the regional social interaction density and public emotional satisfaction. This study provides a quantifiable AI-based guideline integrating technical and humanistic values for the design of empathetic infrastructure in future smart cities.

title: High Bandwidth Frequency Compensated Three Stage Amplifier using Self Cascode and Flipped Voltage Follower
authors: Om Krishna Gupta, Neeta Pandey, Maneesha Gupta
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: This research work enhances the performance of a three stage amplifier. RNMC is incorporated with FVF to enhance the amplifier's gain and phase response. The self cascode based differential amplifier forms the input stage. The feed forward path helps to boost the step response. TSMC 0.18 µm CMOS technology parameters are used in tanner tool simulations to confirm the amplifier’s functionality. The GBW of the proposed amplifier is 28 MHz, and its gain is 104 dB. The amplifier also has a slew rate (SR) of 8.1 V/µS. The figure of merits validates the amplifier's effectiveness.

title: ECC Encoded Robust Digital Watermarking using Edge Detection for Error-Resilient Image Communication
authors: Abhijith S, Sreenidhi Prabha Rajeev
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: In modern digital communication systems, maintaining data integrity and robustness against noise and distortion is essential, particularly in multimedia transmission. This project presents a robust digital image watermarking technique that integrates Error Correction Coding (ECC) with edge-based Least Significant Bit (LSB) embedding to ensure reliable watermark transmission without degrading image quality. Initially, Hamming codes are employed to detect double-bit errors and correct single-bit errors in the embedded watermark data, establishing a foundation for basic error control capability. To enhance reliability further, the system incorporates BCH(15,7,2) coding, which can correct up to two random bit errors per codeword, providing superior performance over conventional Hamming schemes. The encoded watermark is embedded into image edge pixels with the help of Edge Detection Algorithms to achieve both imperceptibility and robustness. Objective metrics such as Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) and Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) are used to evaluate the quality of the stego image. Experimental results demonstrate that integrating ECC with LSB watermarking significantly improves error correction capability while maintaining high visual fidelity. The proposed hybrid MATLAB–Verilog architecture offers a practical, hardware-compatible solution for secure and error-tolerant image communication systems.

title: Comparative Analysis of Low-Power SRAM Architectures for Reliable In-Memory Computing
authors: Md Warish Ali, Rajeevan Chandel
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive study of the 6T, 8T, 9T and 10T Static Random Access Memory (SRAM) bitcells for In-Memory Computing (IMC) under the various storage-node patterns (viz. 00,01,10,11) using a 180nm CMOS process. Results show that IMC behavior is strongly data dependent. For the 01/10 conditions, the 6T cell exhibits the lowest delay but suffers from significantly higher total power dissipation compared to the other architectures, while the 9T and 10T cells maintain balanced delay and substantially lower power. Under the 00/11 patterns, the 8T cell records the highest total power dissipation, whereas the 6T, 9T and 10T cells demonstrate nearly similar and much lower power dissipation. Two of the low-power design techniques (namely, transistor stacking and dual-level voltage scaling) are applied across all architectures. These techniques reduce power in every SRAM cell. However, the relative trends remain consistent, with the 6T cell still consuming the highest power for 01/10 and the 8T cell for 00/11. Although delay increases for all the cells due to both low-power techniques, the overall energy efficiency improves. Power-Delay-Product (PDP) analysis confirms that the 10T cell achieves the best PDP across all conditions, making the 10T architecture the most energy-efficient and reliable candidate for low-power IMC applications.

title: Smart Solar Angle Optimizer with ML Forecasting and IoT Synchronization
authors: Hasara Samarawickrama, Priyanka Prabashwara, Dewhara Abeysinghe, Sithmi Himanshi, Dharshana Kasthurirathna, Nuwan Kodagoda
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: In this work, an intelligent framework is introduced for PV system optimization, which includes solar tracking with mirrors, forecasting of energy production, battery lifetime optimization, and data synchronization through machine learning techniques. For efficient operation, the introduced system uses a dual-axis rotation mirror device to track and reflect sun rays to PV panels while receiving current output and irradiation data, thus avoiding the need to rotate heavy structures and allowing cost efficient modification of the roof mounted structure. A combined machine learning framework using LSTM, Prophet, and ARIMA models provides the forecasts of energy production taking into account environment parameters and historical production data for advanced energy planning and load scheduling. For batteries' State of Charge, State of Health estimation, and runtime forecasting, the Random Forest and LSTM algorithms are used in the battery subsystem. Energy efficient communication system employing NB-IoT technologies and incorporating machine learning for transmission scheduling is used for data synchronization considering weather conditions, battery status, and network performance. Anomaly detection is performed in the system continuously. Experimental results show an improvement of up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$18-25 \%$</tex> energy generation, less than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 \%}$</tex> MAE of energy forecasts, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5 - 2 0 \%}$</tex> increased battery life, and reduced communications energy consumption by 40 %.

title: BottleEdgeNet: A Calibrated Edge-Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) Framework for PET Bottle Quality Inspection in Deposit Return Schemes (DRS)
authors: László Duma, Marcos Daniel Cortijo Mendoza
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Ensuring the cleanliness and structural integrity of PET bottles is a key requirement for the effective operation of Deposit Return Schemes (DRS) in circular economy frameworks. Traditional inspection systems and commercial Reverse Vending Machines (RVMs) often fail to detect subtle defects, leading to high False Acceptance and Rejection Rates. This paper presents BottleEdgeNet, a Calibrated EdgeConvolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework for PET bottle quality inspection that integrates EfficientNet-B0, ResNet-50, and a Google Teachable Machine backbone within a unified, low-power inference pipeline implemented in TensorFlow Lite (FP32/INT8) for CPU-oriented edge inference. The proposed system introduces a class-wise power reweighting calibration layer to stabilize confidence scores and mitigate label-shift effects under heterogeneous lighting and background conditions. A hybrid dataset comprising 7,849 training, 972 validation, and 972 testing images (plus 80 realworld samples) was used to evaluate performance across four operational classes: Clean Intact, Clean Damaged, Dirty Intact, and Dirty Damaged. Internal tests revealed that EfficientNetB0 (FP32) achieved the best accuracy (87.96 %), outperforming ResNet-50 (84.47%) and Teachable Machine (67.45%), with negligible degradation after INT8 quantization. External validation confirmed EfficientNet-B0's generalization stability <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{(} \mathbf{8 7. 5} \boldsymbol{\%})$</tex>, while ResNet- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0}$</tex> prioritized contamination detection and Teachable Machine provided a lightweight, no-code alternative for rapid deployment. To enhance field robustness, improvements should focus on data diversity and controlled illumination rather than model complexity. We report operational decision metrics (FAR/FRR) under argmax decision logic to connect model-level classification behavior with accept/reject decisions. In addition, edge-oriented efficiency is discussed through proxy-based considerations across FP32 and INT8 inference. Together, these analyses strengthen the practical relevance of the proposed framework by linking accuracy and confidence stabilization to DRS throughput, quality control, and deployment feasibility. The results demonstrate that calibrated Edge-CNNs can enable accurate, efficient, and scalable inspection of returnable PET bottles, supporting sustainable and decentralized DRS operations.

title: Multilingual Speech Emotion Recognition with Self-Supervised Representations and Prototypical Networks
authors: Kseniia Kholodkovskaia, Cengiz Tepe
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: It is challenging to recognize emotions from a speaker's voice, especially when people speak different languages. Most Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) systems require massive amounts of data, making them difficult to use for languages with limited resources. While modern self-supervised models can capture sound well, fine-tuning them on new languages often fails because emotions are expressed differently across cultures. To solve this, we propose a hybrid few-shot learning method that combines the intermediate representations (Layer 9) of a frozen multilingual model (XLSR-53) with Prototypical Networks. We compared traditional acoustic features (eGeMAPS) and deep-learned features against the Support Vector Machine classifier and Prototypical Networks. We tested this on a novel 5-class multilingual dataset (Russian, Turkish, Spanish, Dari) using Leave-One-Speaker-Out cross-validation. Our XLSR-53+ Prototypical Networks method achieved an accuracy of 87.35%, significantly outperforming the traditional eGeMAPS+SVM baseline <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{4 2. 0 0 \%) })$</tex>. Our results establish that using prototypical networks with the intermediate layers of multilingual transformers is a highly efficient way to recognize speech emotions across different languages using very little data.

title: A Deep Learning Framework for Multivariate Weather Prediction using Sequential and Hybrid Architectures
authors: K.Praveena, Panthangi Pavithra, Kudelli Lakshmi, Uma Maheswari Rayudu, Naladi Ram Babu, P.Chandra Sekhar Reddy
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Accurate weather prediction is essential for effective agricultural planning, as weather variables exhibit strong temporal dependencies and inter-variable relationships. This paper presents a deep learning framework for multivariate weather prediction using sequential and hybrid learning architectures. The proposed framework evaluates machine learning baselines alongside sequential deep learning models to analyze their capability in modeling multivariate weather time-series data. Recurrent neural network, long short-term memory, and gated recurrent unit models are employed to capture temporal patterns across multiple weather variables. To further enhance prediction performance, hybrid models integrating convolutional neural networks with gated recurrent units and an attention mechanism are developed to jointly learn local temporal patterns and long-range dependencies. Experimental results demonstrate that sequential deep learning models significantly outperform machine learning baselines, while hybrid architectures achieve the lowest prediction error and highest accuracy across all evaluated weather parameters. The results confirm the effectiveness of combining convolutional, gated recurrent, and attention-based learning for robust multivariate weather prediction in agricultural environments.

title: Data-Driven Parametric Energy Optimization of Mecanum Wheeled Mobile Robots on Constrained Trajectories
authors: Anıl Gökhan Göktaş, Semih Sezer
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The deployment of mobile robots in industrial environments characterized by limited space necessitates the implementation of energy optimization strategies to ensure continuous operation. Mecanum wheels are frequently utilized due to their high maneuverability; however, their unique 45° roller structure creates natural internal friction and slippage, resulting in higher energy consumption than standard wheels. This study investigates the parametric optimization of a mecanum-wheeled mobile robot navigating a constrained trajectory defined by strategic obstacles. By employing the Taguchi method to minimize the number of experiments, the parameters of linear velocity, angular velocity, and payload capacity were systematically modified to analyze their impact on total energy consumption. Experimental data was utilized to train machine learning models, implicitly capturing the nonlinear friction losses that are challenging to model analytically. Among the evaluated algorithms, Random Forest yielded the highest prediction accuracy with an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}$</tex>-squared score of 0.903 and a MAPE of 44.97. A simulation environment mirroring the real-world constrained path demonstrated that adaptively adjusting velocity parameters minimize energy consumption successfully. Theoretical energy consumption values aligned closely with predictions, validating the proposed data-driven framework. The findings provide a practical optimization methodology for enhancing the energy efficiency of mecanumwheeled systems under varying payload and velocity conditions in real-world industrial applications.

title: Highly Sensitive Detection of Iron in Water Using an Adaptive Phase Alignment Analog Lock-In Amplifier
authors: Xiao Liu, Jiacheng Liu, Yue Li, Xueji Wang, Jingru Zhang, Tao Yu, Bingliang Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This work presents an adaptive phase alignment analog lock-in amplifier (AP-LIA) integrated into a compact dual-beam spectrophotometric system for highly sensitive iron detection in water. The AP-LIA preserves the hardware simplicity of single-phase lock-in amplifiers while introducing a sinusoidal reference signal combined with an adaptive phase alignment algorithm based on extremum search. By continuously adjusting the reference phase to maximize the demodulated DC output, the system achieves automatic and precise phase synchronization between the signal under test and the reference signal. This design eliminates the harmonic demodulation errors inherent in conventional single-phase architectures and simultaneously avoids the additional hardware complexity and cost associated with dual-phase systems. A compact prototype was developed and coupled with a colorimetric assay to evaluate analytical performance. Validation across seven concentration gradients (10–1000 μg/L) demonstrated excellent linearity, with the AP-LIA-based system achieving a sensitivity of 3.2 × 10⁻⁴ A/(μg/L), a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.9 μg/L, and a limit of quantification (LOQ) of 16.34 μg/L, representing clear improvements over commercial spectrophotometer and previously reported methods. These results confirm the potential of the AP-LIA as a novel strategy for achieving highly sensitive detection in sensor applications that require low power consumption, reduced hardware cost, and simplified circuit complexity.

title: A phase correlation-based time series InSAR algorithm_supp1-3698781.docx
authors: Zhong Lu
publish: N/A
abstract: Coherence loss due to changes in surface scattering properties presents a significant challenge for deformation mapping using Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR). A key objective in time-series InSAR processing is identifying ground targets that remain relatively stable over time. Many existing time-series InSAR algorithms rely on the statistical analysis of individual scatterers. In this study, we introduce a novel phase correlation-based InSAR algorithm that exploits the spatial continuity of time-series phase measurements. We develop a statistical model for the wrapped phase spatial difference and identify Spatially Correlated Pixels (SCPs)—pixels that exhibit strong spatial correlation with neighboring pixels, quantified using temporal coherence of their relative wrapped phase histories. Bayesian inference is employed to select these SCPs, which are then processed using an adaptive filter weighted by the estimated temporal coherence with neighboring pixels. We apply the proposed algorithm to C-band Sentinel-1 data over the Greater Houston Area (GHA) in Texas, United States. The results demonstrate the algorithm’s ability to effectively identify coherent scatterers and retrieve deformation signals even in challenging conditions, offering improved spatial coverage and phase quality.

title: A Sub-electron-noise Skipper-CCD Readout ASIC with Improved Channel-to-channel Isolation and an Integrated Cryogenic Voltage Reference
authors: Fabricio Alcalde Bessia, Claudio Chavez, Troy England, Hongzhi Sun, Andrew Lathrop, Davide Braga, Miguel Sofo-Haro, Juan Estrada, Farah Fahim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The Multi-channel Integrating Dual-slope Noise-Aware (MIDNA) Application Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs) are a series of skipper-CCD readout chips fabricated in a 65nm low-power CMOS process that implement a Correlated Double Sampling (CDS) signal processing chain based on dual-slope integrators. They are designed to operate from room temperature to cryogenic temperatures, down to 84K. The present iteration of the ASIC has been fabricated with several design updates and the addition of an on-chip voltage reference, resulting in improved performance. This work presents the main vulnerabilities solved, the changes carried out, and the resulting performance benefits. Measurements with a skipper-CCD and the ASIC at 140K showed that the single-electron resolution can be reached by averaging the measured charge in the analog domain using the analog pile-up technique with a readout noise as low as 0.11 e<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−</sup><sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</sub> of equivalent charge for 1200 averaged samples. The channel-to-channel crosstalk was characterized, showing an improvement from −38dB in the previous iteration to better than −62dB in the present ASIC. When using the analog pile-up technique, one limiting factor was the residual offset after CDS. In the updated ASIC, improvements to the integrator offset have decreased the CDS residue from 3.2mV to 0.2mV, enabling additional signal pile-up.

title: Extraction of Cardiac Time Intervals from a Radar Kinetocardiogram for Continuous Blood Pressure Monitoring
authors: Jinke Xie, Juhua Huang, Hongtao Wan, Chongnan Xu, Xuetao Zuo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Noncontact blood pressure (BP) monitoring remains challenging. Conventional pulse-wave–based approaches rely on fine-grained waveform morphology and are vulnerable to respiratory interference. Deep learning can improve accuracy, yet often lacks physiological interpretability and requires large training datasets. This work proposes a radar-kinetocardiogram (RKG)–based BP monitoring method that does not rely on deep learning. To suppress respiratory interference, we introduce Adaptive Respiratory-Harmonic Variational Mode Extraction (ARH-VME), which effectively removes respiratory fundamentals and harmonics while preserving key cardiac components. By extracting cardiac time intervals (CTIs) from the RKG and estimating BP primarily from these temporal markers, the method markedly reduces dependence on detailed waveform morphology and improves robustness. In a clinical study with 25 subjects, the approach achieved a mean error (ME), mean absolute error (MAE), and standard deviation (SD) of 0.3 (5.72) ± 6.44 mmHg for systolic BP (SBP) and −0.4 (3.02) ± 3.6 mmHg for diastolic BP (DBP) under static conditions, falling within the numerical limits commonly referenced from the AAMI standard, and demonstrated stable performance over 40–100 cm sensing distances as well as incidence angles up to 45°. Cross-platform generalizability was further validated on an independent public dataset. These results indicate a physiologically interpretable and robust pathway for practical, noncontact BP monitoring.

title: Machine Learning-Aided RF Circuit and Antenna Design: A Review on Emerging Trends and Techniques
authors: Daud Khan, Md Mahabub Alam, Messaoud Ahmed Ouameur, Arif Ullah, Iftikhar Ud Din, Miloud Bagaa, Daniel Massicotte, Bahram Razmpoosh
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Electromagnetics, Antennas and Propagation
abstract: The growing complexity of modern wireless systems, especially for 5G, 6G, and IoT applications, demands efficient and intelligent design approaches for both radio frequency (RF) circuits and antenna systems. Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a powerful enabler that offers data-driven techniques for modeling, optimization, and synthesis of communication hardware. In this paper, we discuss a unified perspective of AI-aided techniques used for RF circuits and antenna design. These techniques mainly encompass a broad range of learning methods, such as supervised learning, deep neural networks, evolutionary algorithms, reinforcement learning, and generative models. The survey highlights their roles in performance prediction, layout optimization, inverse design, and co-design strategies. Unlike existing works that specifically address the RF or antenna domains in terms of isolation, this paper integrates both domains and emphasizes on their joint optimization to meet system-level performance requirements. Comparative analysis, categorized summaries, and a focused discussion on current challenges are discussed that include limited datasets, lack of interpretability, and the gap between simulation and hardware implementation. Furthermore, this paper aims to provide a guide for researchers working on the development of intelligent, scalable, and physically aware AI frameworks for next-generation communication systems.
