title: Contrast-Free Ultrasound Microvascular Imaging via Radiality and Similarity Weighting
authors: Jingyi Yin, Jingke Zhang, Lijie Huang, U-Wai Lok, Ryan M DeRuiter, Kaipeng Ji, Yanzhe Zhao, Kate M. Knoll, Kendra E. Petersen, Tao Wu, Xiang-yang Zhu, James D Krier, Kathryn A. Robinson, Lilach O Lerman, Andrew J. Bentall, Shigao Chen, Chengwu Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Ultrasonics
abstract: Microvascular imaging has advanced significantly with ultrafast data acquisition and improved clutter filtering, enhancing the sensitivity of power Doppler imaging to small vessels. However, the image quality remains limited by spatial resolution and elevated background noise, both of which impede visualization and accurate quantification. To address these limitations, this study proposes a high-resolution cross-correlation power Doppler (HR-XPD) method that integrates spatial radiality weighting with Doppler signal coherence analysis, thereby enhancing spatial resolution while suppressing artifacts and background noise. Quantitative evaluations in simulation and in vivo experiments on healthy human liver, transplanted human kidney, and pig kidney demonstrated that HR-XPD significantly improves microvascular resolvability and contrast compared to conventional PD. In vivo results showed up to a 2 to 3-fold enhancement in spatial resolution and a 10 dB increase in contrast. High-resolution vascular details were clearly depicted within a short acquisition time of only 0.3 s-1.2 s without the use of contrast agents. These findings indicate that HR-XPD provides an effective, contrast-free, and high-resolution microvascular imaging approach with broad applicability in both preclinical and clinical research.

title: Integrating LLMs and Digital Twins for Adaptive Multi-Robot Task Allocation in Construction_supp3-3697962.txt
authors: Xi Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Multi-robot systems are emerging as a promising solution to the growing demand for productivity and safety across industrial sectors. However, effectively coordinating multiple robots in dynamic and uncertain environments, such as construction sites, remains a challenge, particularly due to unpredictable factors like material delays, unexpected site conditions, and weather-induced disruptions. To address these challenges, this study proposes an adaptive task allocation framework that strategically leverages the synergistic potential of Digital Twins, Integer Programming (IP), and Large Language Models (LLMs). The multi-robot task allocation problem is formally modeled using IP, considering task dependencies, robot heterogeneity, scheduling constraints, and replanning needs. A narrative-driven schedule adaptation mechanism is introduced, where unstructured natural language inputs are interpreted by an LLM to autonomously update optimization constraints, enabling human-in-the-loop flexibility without manual programming. A digital twin system enables real-time synchronization between physical operations and their digital representations and serves as the user interface, creating a closed-loop feedback mechanism that keeps the system responsive to site changes. A case study demonstrates both the computational efficiency of the optimization algorithm and the reasoning performance of LLMs, achieving over 97% accuracy in constraint and parameter extraction. The results confirm the practicality, adaptability, and cross-domain applicability of the proposed methods.

title: Towards Smarter Infrastructure: Integrating CS and AI-Driven Edge IoT Systems
authors: Igor Bisio, Chiara Garibotto, Fabio Lavagetto, Andrea Sciarrone, Matteo Zerbino
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Magazine
abstract: Compressive Sensing (CS) has emerged as a modern and alternative signal processing paradigm that challenges the traditional Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem by enabling accurate signal reconstruction from significantly fewer measurements. This paradigm proves particularly valuable in edge-enabled IoT systems, where devices operate under strict constraints in terms of energy, bandwidth, and computational capacity. In this article, we explore the application of CS in edge-enabled IoT scenarios, with a focus on smart infrastructure monitoring. We examine various system architectures and frameworks that leverage AI-based solutions to optimize resource utilization while maintaining high levels of accuracy. However, the mere integration of CS or AI models does not inherently ensure optimal performance. Achieving tangible benefits requires careful co-optimization of communication and computation strategies at the edge, in order to fully harness the energy-saving potential of these technologies in real-world IoT deployments. To this end, the article presents a range of strategies designed to address this challenge, highlighting the effectiveness of combining CS and AI in edge IoT systems and paving the way for more efficient and intelligent resource management in AI-empowered IoT environments.

title: IEEE Industry Applications Society Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics

title: Low-Cost Screen-Printed Flexible Substrate-Integrated Nonradiative Dielectric Waveguide: A Proof of Concept
authors: Mohammed Farouk Nakmouche, Gaozhi Xiao, Mourad Nedil
publish: IEEE Journal of Radio Frequency Identification
abstract: This paper presents, for the first time, a low-cost, flexible Substrate-Integrated Nonradiative Dielectric (SINRD) waveguide. The proposed Flex SINRD operates in the 22-26 GHz frequency band, exhibiting an average insertion loss of approximately -4.78 dB and a return loss better than -20 dB. The impact of key design parameters on the operational bandwidth and transmission performance is analyzed. The Flex SINRD is fabricated using screen-printing technology on a polyetherimide substrate (ε = 3.08, tan δ = 0.00276 at 10 GHz) with a thickness of 0.125 mm. Measurement results from the printed prototypes demonstrate excellent agreement with commercial EM simulations, underscoring the potential of this technology for flexible antenna feeds, conformal arrays, wearable wireless communication systems, or integrated flexible RF front-end applications.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning Enabled Persistent Surveillance with Energy-Aware UAV-UGV Systems for Disaster Management Applications_supp1-3696943.mp4
authors: Md Safwan Mondal
publish: N/A
abstract: Integrating Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) with Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs) provides an effective solution for persistent surveillance in disaster management. UAVs excel at covering large areas rapidly, but their range is limited by battery capacity. UGVs, though slower, can carry larger batteries for extended missions. By using UGVs as mobile recharging stations, UAVs can extend mission duration through periodic refueling, leveraging the complementary strengths of both systems. To optimize this energy-aware UAV-UGV cooperative routing problem, we propose a planning framework that determines optimal routes and recharging points between a UAV and a UGV. Our solution employs a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework built on an encoder-decoder transformer architecture with multi-head attention mechanisms. This architecture enables the model to sequentially select actions for visiting mission points and coordinating recharging rendezvous between the UAV and UGV. The DRL model is trained to minimize the age periods (the time gap between consecutive visits) of mission points, ensuring effective surveillance. We evaluate the framework across various problem sizes and distributions, comparing its performance against heuristic methods and an existing learning-based model. Results show that our approach consistently outperforms these baselines in both solution quality and runtime. Additionally, we demonstrate the DRL policy's applicability in a real-world disaster scenario as a case study and explore its potential for online mission planning to handle dynamic changes. Adapting the DRL policy for priority-driven surveillance highlights the model's generalizability for real-time disaster response. More details are available on our website: https://sites.google.com/view/ecucps.

title: Adaptive Driving Waveform Optimization for Electroluminescent Textile Displays
authors: Kainian Yang, Weichun Chen, Tao Zhou, Shen Huang, Yichi Zhang, Zihao Zhou, Peining Chen, Bo-Ru Yang
publish: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters
abstract: Electroluminescent (EL) textile displays with interlaced architecture have emerged as a promising technology for wearable display applications but generally suffer from limited luminance and poor contrast ratio under conventional driving methods. In this work, suitable AC driving conditions of 120 V and 20 kHz are determined through optical characterization. Based on these conditions, an adaptive driving waveform optimization algorithm is proposed to dynamically adjust driving waveforms according to pattern characteristics. Experimental verification is performed using a pulse amplitude modulation (PAM) driving system. The proposed adaptive method increases the display luminance from 1.15 nit to 4.97 nit (4.3-fold improvement) and enhances the contrast ratio from 7 to 99 (14-fold improvement) for representative patterns, demonstrating significantly improved display performance in EL textile displays.

title: Multi-Vehicle Interaction and State-Aware Temporal Enhanced Graph Attention Network for Lane-Changing Intention Prediction
authors: Yonghai He, Huajie Wan, Changchang Li, Yanli Jiao, Mingmin Han
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Early and reliable recognition of lane changing intention is essential for improving road safety and sustaining efficient freeway operations in intelligent transportation systems. This study develops a state aware processing pipeline for high resolution vehicle trajectories and proposes a Temporal enhanced Graph Attention Network (TE-GAT) for early lane changing intention prediction. Using the real-world drone-based highD freeway dataset as the primary data source, this study first extracts nine longitudinal, lateral, and interaction-related microscopic variables. After entropy-weight analysis and Pearson-correlation screening, six representative variables are retained for traffic-state clustering, leading to three interpretable traffic states: stable free flow, high-speed free flow, and transitional/congested flow. These traffic-state descriptors are then fused as global contextual features in TE-GAT, which augments interaction-aware graph attention with temporal positional encoding and lightweight one-dimensional convolutions. To address class imbalance in lane-changing events, the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique is applied only to the training set after the train-test split. The proposed framework is evaluated against representative baselines, including LSTM, GCN, and vanilla GAT-based models, across multiple modeling horizons. Results show that a modeling window of approximately 1.0 s before lane-change onset provides the best empirical trade-off between anticipation time and classification robustness. Quantitative evaluations demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves a peak accuracy of 97.80% at this optimal 1.0 s prediction horizon with traffic-state fusion. The model also obtains F1 scores above 98% for lane-changing maneuvers, outperforming the baseline methods under the same experimental setting. By enabling earlier and smoother responses of advanced driver assistance and connected automated driving systems, the proposed method can help reduce critical lane changing conflicts, mitigate stop and go oscillations, and support safer and more sustainable freeway traffic operations.

title: Advancing Multi-Robot Task Allocation: A Comprehensive Review of Emerging Trends and Challenges
authors: Saddam I. D. Bakhat, Inam Ullah, Xiaoshan Bai, Weidong Zhang, Jianqiang Li, Shuzhi Sam Ge
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This survey advances understanding of Multi-Robot Task Allocation (MRTA), a foundational problem in robotics for optimizing fleet performance across diverse scenarios. We propose a unified formulation that integrates taxonomies and equation-level templates, capturing core variants such as instantaneous/time-extended allocation (IA/TA), single/multi-robot tasks (SR/MR), and single/multi-task robots (ST/MT), and extending to stochastic and human-aware scenarios. Surveying algorithmic landscapes, we cover optimization-based techniques, market/auction heuristics, and learning innovations (Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)/Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) with centralized training, decentralized execution, and attention encoders), emphasizing post-2021 hybrids for dynamic environments. We provide in-depth analyses of challenges such as scalability in swarms, real-time decision-making under uncertainty, communication robustness, and safety, supported by comparative tables and practitioner cheat sheets. Unlike prior work, our review prioritizes progress metrics, limitations, and deployment selection criteria. Looking forward, we propose directions in AI-augmented MRTA (e.g., LLM-driven orchestration, edge-enabled verification), federated learning for decentralization, and human-aware ethics, offering a strategic roadmap for scalable, intelligent multi-robot coordination with societal and industrial impact.

title: Machine Learning Unveils System-Level Signatures of Integrated Motor&#x2013;Non-Motor Dysfunction in Parkinson&#x2019;s Disease during Telerehabilitation_supp3-3697696.docx
authors: LUCIA PEPA
publish: N/A

title: Robust Peak Shaving in Residential PV–Battery Systems Using Forecast-Free Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ahmed Chiheb Ammari, Amine Lahyani, Majdi Mansouri
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Residential photovoltaic (PV) systems with behind-the-meter battery energy storage systems can reduce electricity costs under time-of-use and demand-charge tariffs. However, effective control remains challenging because monthly demand charges are often determined by short-duration on-peak grid-import events rather than by average energy consumption. This challenge is further amplified by residential load variability, uncertain PV generation, and nonstationary peak timing, under which forecast-dependent controllers may degrade after deployment. Existing rule-based and forecast-driven strategies also remain sensitive to manually tuned rules, forecasting errors, and deployment shifts. This paper proposes a forecast-free reinforcement learning controller for residential PV–battery energy management, explicitly designed for robust peak shaving under demand-charge tariffs. The controller operates in closed loop using only instantaneous measurements and time features, without load or PV forecasts. It employs demand-aware reward shaping to penalize incremental on-peak grid-import increases while preserving battery flexibility for late critical peaks. This design reduces reliance on forecast quality and manually tuned dispatch rules. The proposed method is evaluated against optimization-based baselines, including model predictive control with perfect information and model predictive control with simple persistence forecasts, within a train-once, evaluate-many framework that separates nominal performance from robustness under deployment shifts. The evaluation covers nominal operation, daily stress-day scenarios, structured perturbations in demand and peak-window definitions, and monthly billing-period replay. Under nominal conditions, the proposed controller reduces the mean daily total cost by 20.3% relative to the practical forecast-based MPC baseline. Under stress-day and deployment-shift conditions, it consistently remains the practical controller closest to the perfect-information benchmark, with particularly strong performance when critical peaks occur late in the on-peak window. At the monthly billing horizon, it reduces the mean monthly billing peak by 28.43% relative to the practical forecast-based MPC baseline. Distributional analysis further confirms a systematic downward shift in monthly bill outcomes rather than improvements limited to a few favorable cases. These results demonstrate that the proposed forecast-free reinforcement learning controller provides a practical and deployment-oriented solution for robust residential peak shaving under demand-charge tariffs.

title: AraEduAgent: Arabic EduAgent Dataset for Peer-Attention Modeling in eLearning
authors: Areej A. Bawazir, Asmaa K. AlShumrani, Reem A. Qaid, Walaa S. AlFahmi, Mourad Mars
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This work presents AraEduAgent, the first Arabic adaptation of the EduAgent benchmark for modeling peer-attention effects in online learning environments. EduAgent combines multimodal behavioral signals, cognitive state annotations, student demographics, and post-lecture assessments to enable fine-grained simulation of student learning behaviors. However, prior work has focused exclusively on English data, limiting applicability in Arabic educational contexts. We construct AraEduAgent by translating all textual components Areas of Interest (AoIs), lecture transcripts, demographic descriptions, and assessment questions using a machine-assisted pipeline with dual annotation and inter-annotator agreement to ensure linguistic fidelity. Using the Arabic dataset, we reproduce the original EduAgent experiments and benchmark five large language models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o Mini, Llama 3.1, Qwen2.5 7B, and ALLaM 7B) on behavioral, cognitive, and assessment metrics. Our results reveal that GPT-4o Mini achieves the strongest overall performance, demonstrating high real simulated consistency and rapid few-shot learning, while Arabic-optimized models such as Qwen2.5 7B outperform larger general-purpose models on cognitive state prediction. Notably, ALLaM-7B despite operating under prompt compression due to its limited context window achieves competitive confusion prediction, ranking second among all models, demonstrating the advantage of Arabic-specialized pretraining for cognitive state modeling. Cross-linguistic analysis shows a persistent Arabic English performance gap, though this gap narrows substantially for more capable multilingual models. AraEduAgent provides the first behavior-rich Arabic benchmark for peer-attention modeling, establishing a foundation for developing attention-aware and adaptive educational systems for Arabic-speaking learners. The dataset, simulation, and benchmarking frameworks are publicly released at https://github.com/areejadel/AraEduAgent.

title: A Radix-2/4/8-based Pipelined NTT/INTT Architecture for Homomorphic Encryption
authors: Ju-Hyung Lee, Young-Chan Jang, Kyung-Wook Shin
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Homomorphic encryption (HE) is a cryptographic scheme that enables data to be stored, transmitted, and processed in encrypted form, making it an effective solution for data-sensitive applications such as defense and healthcare. In HE schemes, polynomial multiplication dominates computational complexity and can be efficiently accelerated using the number theoretic transform (NTT). Since the NTT has a mathematical structure similar to that of the discrete Fourier transform (DFT), fast Fourier transform (FFT) algorithms can be similarly applied. This paper proposes a hardware implementation method that maps a VLSI-oriented radix-2/4/8 FFT algorithm onto the NTT. The proposed radix-2/4/8 algorithm exhibits excellent scalability across a wide range of polynomial degrees N (1024≤N≤32768), offering a significant performance advantage over fixed-radix NTT alternatives. It introduces multiple trivial multiplications with fixed multiplicands and exhibits a regular butterfly structure. To maximize the benefits of these trivial multiplications, the Shoup modular multiplication (SMM) algorithm, optimized for fixed multiplicand modular multiplication, is employed to reduce hardware computational cost. Furthermore, in the first stage of the radix-2/4/8 NTT, all modular multiplications in butterfly operations are performed with fixed multiplicands. Based on this property, we propose two processing element (PE) architectures: one applies SMM to all modular multiplications, and the other combines SMM with Barrett modular multiplication (BMM). To demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed radix-2/4/8 algorithm, NTT/INTT hardware accelerators utilizing an 8-parallel pipelined multi-path delay commutator (MDC) architecture are designed for polynomial degrees of N=4096 and 1024. Experimental results on Xilinx FPGA devices (Virtex-7 and Virtex UltraScale+) demonstrate that our designs achieve 1.08×-16.03× higher area-time product (AT) performance compared with state-of-the-art designs.

title: A Model Predictive Hybrid Observer for Current Sensorless PFC Operation of Three-Phase Three-Level Vienna Rectifier
authors: Tawfique Uzzaman, Tyrone Fernando, Herbert HoChing Iu, Xinan Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The robust and resilient model predictive control (MPC) of grid-interfaced AC-DC converters plays an important role for micro-/smart-grids, where parameter uncertainties, power factor correction, and DC-link imbalance are critical requirements. The MPC-oriented control framework for a three-phase three-level Vienna rectifier (3P3L-VR) represents an effective topology for obtaining regulated DC-link voltage and unity power factor. Three current sensors are generally required for voltage-oriented control methods, driving up the cost, circuit complexity and input current total harmonic distortion (THD). To solve such problems, this paper proposes a current-sensorless control method for the 3P3L-VR based on an improved hybrid observer. To accurately estimate the input currents from voltage measurement, the proposed hybrid observer utilizes a model predictive current reconstruction (MPCR) approach with an improved linear extended state observer (LESO). The improved LESO uses a recursive least square (RLS) algorithm to update its gains against the system disturbances and parameter variations. The MPCR approach introduces an adaptive predictive current reconstruction method, with RLS employed for parameter adaptation. Integrating the proposed hybrid observer into the 3P3L-VR controller enhances transient response, improves estimation robustness, and mitigates the overall input current THD. The superior performance of the proposed method in comparison with the state-of-the-art control approaches is validated by experimental results.

title: RWTCbot: A New Wall-Climbing Robot Capable of Wall-to-Wall Transitions at Internal and External Right Angles in All Scenarios_supp1-3699199.mp4
authors: Jianhua Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: This letter introduces RWTCbot, a magnetic-adhesion wall-climbing robot capable of performing all types of internal and external right-angle wall transitions. The robot is equipped with an auxiliary adhesion module that provides adjustable magnetic support and a passive rotary joint that allows relative rolling between robotic front and rear sections. Building on these features, we developed a control algorithm that enables RWTCbot to automatically navigate all types of right-angle transitions, including challenging cases such as moving between walls and ceilings or between two walls that are perpendicular to each other during horizontal travel tasks. A static analysis of RWTCbot's wall-transition behavior was conducted, and comprehensive experimental tests across all scenarios confirmed its reliable transition performance.

title: Explainable Spatio-Temporal Graph Modeling for Network Attack Detection
authors: Mehmet Akif Gulum, Tan Gürpinar, Jason Turner, Omer Dogan
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Modern enterprise networks exhibit highly dynamic and relational traffic patterns, where malicious activities often emerge as subtle spatio-temporal behaviors rather than isolated anomalies. While graph-based deep learning models have shown promise for network attack detection, most approaches provide limited insight into why an alert is triggered or which entities are responsible. We propose an explainable spatio-temporal graph learning framework for network attack detection and forensic analysis. Network traffic is modeled as a sequence of temporal graphs and processed using a graph convolutional network combined with a gated recurrent unit to capture spatial dependencies and temporal evolution. To explain detection decisions, we use Integrated Gradients to provide unified attribution across nodes, time steps, and traffic features from a single explanation signal. The framework is validated in a controlled synthetic environment with exact ground truth and evaluated on the Los Alamos National Laboratory enterprise network dataset. Across 704 attack windows, explanations remain sparse and causally meaningful, with ablation of identified attacker nodes yielding an average attack-logit drop of 29.61. These results demonstrate that the proposed approach enables reliable detection while producing actionable explanations suitable for real-world security operations.

title: An Agentic-Inspired Approach to the Recycling Environmental Challenge
authors: Laci Parker, Mark Allison
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Improper recycling remains a significant contributor to environmental degradation, landfill overflow, and contamination of recyclable materials. Characterized as a grand societal challenge, mitigating pollution through recycling has seen slight improvement despite intensified efforts. One contributing factor is contamination from recycling driven by confusion at the point of disposal. This paper introduces a multimodal AI agent designed to ameliorate the proper classification of household waste and improve consumer-level recycling decisions. Our Agentic AI approach integrates deterministic rules, large language model (LLM) reasoning, and image captioning via Bootstrapping LanguageImage Pre-training (BLIP), which supports natural language and visual inputs. Classification decisions are grounded in and constrained by city-specific composting policy and contextualized reasoning. For proof of concept, four evaluation datasets were constructed to assess the assistant's performance across text, image, and hybrid input modes. After some refinement, the system achieved 96% accuracy on both refined 130-item combined datasets, 100% accuracy on a text-only set, and 92% accuracy on an image-only set. Results demonstrate that integrating structured heuristics with LLM-based fallback reasoning enables robust, transparent classification across diverse, ambiguous waste inputs. This work contributes to applied research in explainable AI, sustainable computing, and human-centered intelligent agents.

title: A Study on Handwritten Text Recognition and Post-OCR Error Correction Techniques
authors: Ashlin Deepa R N, Peta Sravani, Thirupathi Aarthi, Matam Shreshta, Atul Negi, C. Manvitha Reddy
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) of handwritten Telugu text remains a challenging task due to complex characters in the Telugu script and variable handwriting styles. Traditional OCR systems that use handcrafted features have shown limited performance on such data. Deep learning methods outperform traditional OCR methods because they learn patterns directly from images instead of depending on fixed rules. These methods also produce errors due to the visual similarity between the intra-class characters, variations in handwriting styles, and image quality. The introduction of post-OCR error correction techniques helped to eliminate these issues. In this post-OCR approach, the text which contains errors is generated by deep learning–based OCR models, is given as input to a post-OCR model that processes and corrects the incorrect text to produce a final corrected output. This survey focuses on OCR and post-OCR error correction methods and discusses their evaluated performance. The study is mainly towards Indic languages, and it shows that post-OCR models help improve performance.

title: Self-Adaptive Frequency Enhancement with Hierarchical Spatial-Temporal Transformers for Embryo Developmental Stage Classification
authors: Soundhiri S, Dhivya P, Rajesh Kanna P, Srisoundaryaa S S P
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Improving the success rates of assisted reproductive technologies: Time-lapse microscopy is an automated embryo assessment strategy necessary to enhance the success rate of assisted reproductive technologies. But the poor contrast, nonuniform light and noise are common to embryo photos and hence, both manual and traditional automatic classification becomes challenging. This work has the objective of developing a deep learning framework that will reliably and clearly distinguish between the developmental stages of embryos as either excellent or bad. Some of the objectives include enhancing the quality of images in real-time, precise segmentation of embryos, spatiotemporal features, and reliable classification. Existing methods, like the classical filtering and the CNN-based ones often blur out minor morphological features or cannot process the dynamics of embryos, which reduces their readability and reliability. This study uses publicly available collection of microscopic embryo pictures which is divided into training, validation, and testing images and includes pictures that are labeled as good (1) and not good (0). The proposed methodology initially uses a new Self-Adaptive Frequency Enhancement (SAFE) preprocessing approach in order to enhance embryo structures. It is then succeeded by hierarchical swin-UNet Transformer and segmentation, and feature extraction and classification by hierarchical Spatio-Temporal Attention Transformer (HSTAT). The framework, according to experimental data, is more accurate at classifying the embryos and estimating the number of good and bad embryos. It also provides attention-driven visualizations that give emphasis on key geographical points and time points that make decisions. Overall, the proposed solution offers a stable, understandable, and efficient method of automated assessment of embryo quality; subsequent studies will focus on the multimodal analysis of data and real-time measurement as well as clinical trials.

title: An Intelligent Q-Learning-Based Telematic Routing Protocol for Energy and Delay Optimization in Underwater Sensor Networks
authors: Sivasakthiselvan S, D Risha Jane, Fazil Shaik
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: The underwater sensor networks (UWSNs) must contend with extremely scarce amounts of energy, extremely high propagation delays and unreliable acoustic channels, which complicates the routing significantly. QTAR and other Q-learning-based routing protocols render networks more energy-saving and less likely to be slowed down, but do not consider the altering link circumstances as well as network congestion that make them less helpful in congested underwater regions. As a solution to this drawback, this paper suggests a QTAR-TEL, an intelligent Q-learning-based telematic routing protocol, which takes into consideration the link quality and queue awareness during the routing decision-making. The proposed protocol retains the topology knowledgeable structure of QTAR as well as enhance the reward functionality in such a manner as to be able to maximize the energy consumption, latency, and reliability of packet delivery simultaneously. QTAR-TEL is simulated using a Python-based simulation framework and again simulated using MATLAB-based simulations on networks containing up to 300 nodes. Comparative analysis of energy consumption per packet, end-to-end delay, packet delivery ratio and network lifetime reveal that the QTAR-TEL protocol has been continuously performing better compared to the baseline QTAR protocol in reducing energy consumption, delay and enhancing delivery reliability and network lifetime.

title: Optimizing Sustainable Water Management and Renewable Energy Generation Through Floating Photovoltaics and Artificial Intelligence
authors: Mustafa Ihsan Falih, Oguz Karan
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The growing challenges of water scarcity and the imperative to transition towards renewable energy sources have become central concerns in the global quest for sustainability. This paper investigates the optimization of a floating photovoltaic (PV) system on the Euphrates River using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), a reinforcement learning algorithm. The primary objective was to enhance the system's efficiency by optimizing the placement and orientation of the PV panels in response to dynamic environmental conditions. The study employed a comprehensive simulation framework that integrated real-world data from the Iraqi National Weather Agency and the “Sunshine in Asian Cities” dataset, and utilized MATLAB, Simulink, and Autodesk Maya for modeling and analysis. The results demonstrate significant improvements in the performance of the floating PV system after the application of PPO. The average voltage output increased by 9.2 %, from 325 V to 355 V, and the average power output rose by 15.3 %, from 85 kW to 98 kW. These enhancements underscore the effectiveness of PPO in dynamically optimizing PV systems, leading to increased energy generation and improved system efficiency. This research highlights the potential of machine learning techniques in advancing the design and deployment of renewable energy systems, particularly in challenging environments like water bodies.

title: Domain Adaptation in HTR through Self-Supervised Visual Pretraining
authors: Ashlin Deepa R N, Preetham Janugam, Rahul Sangubotla, SK. Jani Pasha, Rambakka Rohith
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Obtaining fully annotated datasets for handwritten text recognition in low-resource regional languages such as Telugu remains a significant bottleneck, primarily due to the extensive manual effort and linguistic expertise required for labeling. To address this limitation, the present study introduces a self-supervised learning framework capable of automatically annotating unlabeled handwritten Telugu text images. The proposed methodology employs self-supervised pretext tasks to derive semantically rich feature representations directly from unlabeled data, which are subsequently utilized to generate high-confidence pseudo-labels for downstream classification. A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a SimCLR backbone serves as the core architecture, trained jointly on both labeled and pseudo-labeled samples to enhance generalization and robustness. Experimental evaluations validate that the proposed framework substantially minimizes manual annotation requirements while preserving competitive recognition accuracy. The results underscore the efficacy of self-supervised learning in developing scalable, cost-effective, and high-performance handwritten text recognition systems for resource-constrained languages such as Telugu.

title: Continual Learning-based Adaptive Urban Flood Prediction with Concept Drift Detection for Smart Cities
authors: Sathiyamoorthy M
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Most of the systems constructed to perform flood forecasting in urban places usually use fixed machine learning models, which cannot recognize the changing conditions of the environment with climate change, urbanization, and the development of infrastructure. This eventually destroys over time particularly in the case of streaming sensor data and concept drift across time. And in this respect, this paper aims at providing a solution to this by proposing an adaptive flood prediction framework in the prototype scale using continual learning and integrating with real-time concept drift detection. The other system is based on statistical drift detection algorithms (ADWIN, Page-Hinkley tests) in order to identify any notable shift in the distributions of rainfall and water level, CPU utilization, flight packages or any other data stream as in the case of drainage. In the case of a drift, the suggested framework can guarantee predictive consistency, and re-training expenses will be spared by performing an incremental online update of model parameters. The architecture integrates both temporal modeling with LSTM and adaptive weighting of recent observations facilitating the system to obtain good performance in dynamic conditions. It is experimentally confirmed that on actual urban hydrological data, the proposed approach can significantly outperform conventional static models in accuracy and resilience and needs significantly less computation. This flexible system is capable of providing a multifaceted scalability as well as flexible response to the real-time flood early warning systems in the smart cities.

title: Support Vector Machine Techniques for Cervical Cancer Risk Assessment
authors: Ariya S Kurup, Thirumaraiselvan P, Duraichi N, Chandralekha S, Gangadharan S, Sathishkumar V E
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Cervical cancer is a major cause of cancer-related death in women globally, with early identification being essential for successful treatment and enhanced survival rates. This research assesses the efficacy of Support Vector Machine (SVM) methodologies for automated cervical cancer risk evaluation using the Cervical Cancer Risk Classification dataset from Kaggle, including 858 patient records with 36 demographic, behavioral, and clinical variables. Preprocessing procedures, including the management of missing values, encoding of categorical variables, and feature scaling, were implemented to prepare the data for classification. Different SVM kernels, including linear, polynomial, and radial basis function (RBF), were used and evaluated to assess their prediction efficacy. Experimental findings indicate that the RBF kernel attained the maximum accuracy of 98.96%, with a precision of 98.76%, a recall of 99.17%, and an F 1 -score of 98.96%, surpassing other kernels in differentiating high-risk from low-risk instances. The ROC-AUC study further validates the robust discriminative capacity of the SVM models. The results indicate that SVM-based models may function as dependable decision-support instruments for the early diagnosis of cervical cancer, possibly aiding healthcare professionals in risk assessment and clinical decision-making. Future research may investigate ensemble methodologies and hybrid feature selection strategies to augment forecast accuracy.

title: X-EyeNet: An AI-based System for Diabetic Retinopathy Severity Grading using Retinal Fundus Images
authors: Adarsh Sharma, Sugavasi Shashank Sai, S Vidhya
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is one of the leading causes of preventable blindness worldwide. The increasing global prevalence of diabetes has intensified the need for reliable automated screening systems, which analyse the severity of the disease based on the fundus images of the retina. Manual evaluation consumes a lot of time and is also susceptible to variabilities, which is why there is a need for the automated solutions. In this paper, we propose a deep learning framework for the automated grading of the severity of the disease known as X-EyeNet. The framework consists of an independent external-eye screening module for preliminary abnormality detection and a fundus-based module for diabetic retinopathy severity grading. As the final severity grade is the analysis of the fundus, for efficient feature extraction, the fundus is pre-processed, and gross analysis is done. The grade is accordingly decided using the disease with the aid of CORAL from the deep learning frameworks. The performance of the model is optimized using the Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK) measure, which is a measure of ordinal agreement in DR severity grading. To demonstrate the exceptional performance with regards to the baseline methods of regression, the results of the implemented X-EyeNet and the review of the APTOS 2019 Blindness Detection datasets, in terms of the QWK to be 0.887 will be shown. X-EyeNet performed strongly in the running by revealing that excessively fine tuning and testing time augmentation weakened the performance and the results strongly maintained the ordinal design with a frozen backbone. The results therefore confirmed that X-EyeNet offered a reliable, and an automated DR severity assessment.

title: Automated Classification of Images of Venomous and Non-Venomous Snakes using Deep Learning
authors: Sanjith Suresh Kumar, Sushma Krishna, Gowri Srinivasa
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Snakes are elongated, limbless reptiles that belong to the phylum Serpentes. It is difficult for non-experts to tell the difference between venomous and non-venomous snakes based on their visual traits, such as head shape, body structure, skin texture, and eye shape. Traditional machine learning methods for snake classification depend on features that are extracted by hand, which restricts both scalability and accuracy. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a deep convolutional neural network (CNN)-based approach for binary classification of snake images into venomous and non-venomous categories. A dataset consisting of 2,793 snake images is used to train and evaluate six neural network models along with the proposed architecture. Data augmentation techniques are applied to increase dataset diversity and improve generalization. In addition, transfer learning is used to further enhance classification performance. Experimental evaluation using five-fold cross-validation with Adam optimization with cosine annealing learning-rate scheduling demonstrates that the proposed model achieves a classification accuracy of 98.516 %.

title: A Cost Effective Smart Electric Vehicle for Safe Indoor Mobility Applications
authors: V.S. Arulmurugan, K.Vidhya, D. Saravanakumar, P.Soundhar
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Electric vehicle (EV) production based on indoor mobility for reliable operation, improve battery lifecycle and advance charging approaches, while decreasing environmental impact. Existing EV based Indoor Mobility approaches have limitations such as unstable low-voltage grid input source, load demand is always high, charging time high, and low recycle in battery condition prediction. To solve these issues, this work propose a Hybrid Adaptive Energy Optimization Algorithm (HAEOA) to improve charging stability, enhance forecasting accuracy, and power quality output. The Input CSV dataset contains EV battery voltage, current, temperature, speed, and charging cycle limits. Input preprocessing applies adaptive normalization with Kalman filtering to reduce noise and improve signal reliability. Deep Time-based Convolutional Network (DTCN) Feature selection is performed using Mutual Information-based Responsive Feature Range to extract the most significant battery and energy variables. A HAEOA is then applied to extract temporal features from EV operational data. Performance is evaluated using Accuracy 96.75%, Precision 97%, Recall 98 %, RMSE 0.565 %, and an MAPE 3.25% and SoC and SoH parameters are evaluated. Results demonstrate improved energy efficiency, enhanced battery performance, and cost effective for safe indoor EV operation.
